Sample DHCP relay with Ubuntu DHCP Server Configuration

Update and install the DHCP server:

Public

VXLAN/EVPN symmetric IRB distributed L3 gateways e... 89

sudo apt get update

sudo apt-get install isc-dhcp-server -y
Editing dhcpd.conf

sudo vi /etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf

option domain-name "local.******.net";

option domain-name-servers 8.8.8.8, 8.8.8.4;

authoritative;

subnet 192.168.3.240 netmask 255.255.255.248 {}

# isc-dhcp-server requires local subnet on DHCP server to be configured in

order to start

            # Here are 2 example DHCP scopes

            # Routers and dhcp-server-identifier would match Active Gateway

IP(and/or IPv6)on the SVIs

subnet 10.1.10.0 netmask 255.255.255.0 {

  range 10.1.10.10 10.1.10.40;

  option routers 10.1.10.1;

  option dhcp-server-identifier 10.1.10.1;

}

subnet 10.1.11.0 netmask 255.255.255.0 {

  range 10.1.11.10 10.1.11.40;

  option routers 10.1.11.1;

  option dhcp-server-identifier 10.1.11.1;

}

# save and exit editor

VRFs with overlapping subnets

sudo vi /etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf

option domain-name "local.******.net";

option domain-name-servers 8.8.8.8, 8.8.8.4;

authoritative;

subnet 192.168.3.240 netmask 255.255.255.248 {}
# isc-dhcp-server requires local subnet on DHCP server to be configured in

order to start

            # The AOS-CX DHCP relay VTEP will send VRF name as part of

option 82, sub-option 151

class "VRF1"

{

    match if substring (option agent.unknown-151,1,100) = "VRF1";

}

class "VRF2"

{

    match if substring (option agent.unknown-151,1,100) = "VRF2";

}

Public

VXLAN/EVPN symmetric IRB distributed L3 gateways e... 90

subnet 10.1.10.0 netmask 255.255.255.0 {

  option routers 10.1.10.1;

  pool {

   allow members of “VRF1”;

   range 10.1.10.10 10.1.10.40;

  }

  pool {

   allow members of “VRF2”;

   range 10.1.10.41 10.1.10.60;

  }

}

# save and exit editor

Start and check DHCP server status

sudo systemctl start isc-dhcp-server.service

sudo systemctl status isc-dhcp-server.service
Additional configuration required on a VTEP for DHCP relay, ensure network connectivity works between
Lo0 and DHCP server through VRF default

interface loopback 0

 ip address 192.168.1.3/32

!

ip source-interface dhcp_relay interface loopback0

dhcp-relay option 82 source-interface

! The above commands ensure that VTEP DHCP relay communication uses Lo0 to

DHCP server

! and DHCP-relay option 82, sub-option 5, 11 and 151 are sent to the DHCP

server using Lo0 IP

!

interface vlan10

 vrf attach VRF1

 ip address 10.1.10.2/24
 ip helper-address 192.168.3.242 vrf default

! Point to DHCP server in VRF default

 active-gateway ip mac 12:00:00:00:01:00

 active-gateway ip 10.1.10.1

!

interface vlan11

 vrf attach VRF2

 ip address 10.1.11.2/24

 ip helper-address 192.168.3.242 vrf default

! Point to DHCP server in VRF default

 active-gateway ip mac 12:00:00:00:01:00

 active-gateway ip 10.1.11.1

Public

VXLAN/EVPN symmetric IRB distributed L3 gateways e... 91

A packet capture of the interaction between the DHCP relay VTEP and DHCP server is shown below with
option 82 and sub-options 5, 11 and 151 with VRF1 in the contents of VRF name field.

Sample DHCP relay with Windows DHCP Server Configuration

The Windows Server 2016 DHCP server requires VTEP loopbacks to be added as scopes that are excluded
from DHCP allocation. For example, Scope 10.1.2.0 and 10.1.1.0 are IP ranges assigned to VTEP loopbacks.

If VTEP loopback scopes are not added, DHCP clients would not be able to correctly grab DHCP IPs.

Public

VXLAN/EVPN symmetric IRB distributed L3 gateways e... 92

Windows Server 2016 DHCP server does not require additional  dhcp-server-identifier  option
to be added when compared to Ubuntu DHCP server.

Use case— IPv6 EVPN VXLAN

This section provides information on use cases supported on AOS-CX for IPv6 EVPN VXLAN. In these
examples, single fabric refers to a VXLAN fabric/VXLAN domain with fully meshed VTEPs/VXLAN Tunnel
End Points.

Use case—IPv4 underlay with dual stack IPv4/IPv6 overlay (single fabric)

The topologies shown inthe following figures will be used to describe this use case.

IPv4 underlay

Public

Use case— IPv6 EVPN VXLAN 93

Figure 1. Use case #1 topology – IPv4 underlay

For the IPv4 underlay:

VTEPs in the fabric are typically 6400 and 8360 Switch series with VSX (dual control plane) or 6300 with
VSF (single control plane).

•

IBGP EVPN RRs/Route Reflectors are typically 8325, 8325H, 8360, 6400, 8400, or 9300 Switch series.

•  Border VTEPs are 8360 Switch series.

•  OSPFv2 is used to advertise and learn Loopbacks between VTEPs and route reflectors (RRs).

•

IPv4 BGP EVPN peering is established between Loopbacks on VTEPs and RRs.

•  VSX VTEPs utilize unique Lo0 for EVPN control plane and anycast Lo1 for data plane tunnel formation

•  VSF VTEPs utilize Lo0 for EVPN control plane and data plane tunnel formation

•  EVPN control plane: VTEPs do not need to peer with other VTEPs, they only need to peer with EVPN

RRs

•  VXLAN data plane: full mesh VXLAN tunnels between VTEPs in a fabric are formed when they

interested in the same VNI/VXLAN Network Identifier

The following CLI output includes configuration examples for EVPN RR1. EVPN RR2 will have a similar
configuration with different IPs.

hostname RR1

!

! downlinks towards VTEPs

interface 1/1/25

no shutdown

mtu 9198
routing
ip mtu 9198

ip address 192.168.3.0/31

ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

Public

Use case— IPv6 EVPN VXLAN 94

ip ospf network point-to-point

interface 1/1/26

no shutdown

mtu 9198

routing

ip mtu 9198

ip address 192.168.3.2/31

ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

ip ospf network point-to-point

!

! Lo0 used for EVPN peering with VTEPs

interface loopback 0

ip address 192.168.1.251/32

ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

!

!

router ospf 1

router-id 192.168.1.251

area 0.0.0.0

router bgp 65001

bgp router-id 192.168.1.251

neighbor VTEP peer-group

neighbor VTEP remote-as 65001

neighbor VTEP update-source loopback 0

neighbor 192.168.1.1 peer-group VTEP

neighbor 192.168.1.2 peer-group VTEP

neighbor 192.168.1.3 peer-group VTEP

neighbor 192.168.1.4 peer-group VTEP

neighbor 192.168.1.5 peer-group VTEP

address-family l2vpn evpn

neighbor leaf route-reflector-client

neighbor leaf send-community extended
neighbor 192.168.1.1 activate

neighbor 192.168.1.2 activate

neighbor 192.168.1.3 activate

neighbor 192.168.1.4 activate

neighbor 192.168.1.5 activate

config with different IPs.

hostname VTEP1

virtual-mac 02:00:00:00:01:00

! A unique virtual-mac is required on all symmetric IRB distributed L3

gateway VTEPs

Use case— IPv6 EVPN VXLAN 95

should be used

vlan 10-11

! Southbound host facing ports

no shutdown

router ospf 1

Dual stack IPv4/IPv6 overlay

Figure 2. Use case #1 topology – dual stack IPv4/IPv6 overlay

Public

Use case— IPv6 EVPN VXLAN 96

For the dual stack IPv4/IPv6 overlay with IPv6 unicast traffic forwarding:

•  Distributed L3 Gateways are deployed on VTEPs when the same VLAN needs to be extended across

multiple VTEPs, while providing optimal routing between hosts on different subnets on the same VTEP.

•  Hosts can utilize static IPv6 IPs, or SLAAC for dynamic IPv6 assignment.

•  The same link local fe80::1/64 address can be used, as the IPv6 default gateway on different VLANs/

SVIs as link local addresses are locally significant.

•  On the border VTEP, OSPF or EBGP is typically used for dynamic route exchange between the VXLAN

fabric and the external/non VXLAN networks.

•  The Int VXLAN 1 tunnel source uses IPv4.

The following CLI output includes configuration examples for VTEP1. Other VTEPs will have a similar
configuration, but with different IP addresses.

hostname VTEP1

!

vrf VRF1

rd 192.168.1.100:1

route-target export 65001:1 evpn

route-target import 65001:1 evpn
evpn

vlan 10

rd auto

route-target export auto

route-target import auto

redistribute host-route

vlan 11

rd auto

route-target export auto

route-target import auto

redistribute host-route

! redistribute host-route is used to advertise host routes (/32 for

Public

Use case— IPv6 EVPN VXLAN 97

IPv4, /128 for IPv6)

! to remote VTEPs

! e.g. 10.1.10.100/32 and/or IPv6 address 2001:db8:1010::100/128 is

connected to VTEP1

! This is required as the same subnet is spread across multiple VTEPs

! Without host routes, a remote VTEP would not be aware of the correct

destination VTEP

! to send traffic

interface vlan 10

vrf attach VRF1

interface vxlan 1 mode ipv6

! IPv4 tunnel source

source ip 192.168.1.100

no shutdown

vni 10010

vlan 10

vni 10011

vlan 11

vni 100001

routing

vrf VRF1

! L3 VNI with VRF attached

IPv6 underlay

Figure 3. Use Case#2 topology – IPv6 underlay

For the IPv6 underlay:

•  VTEPs in the fabric are typically 6400, 8100, or 8360 Switch series with VSX (dual control plane) or

6300 with VSF (single control plane).

Public

Use case— IPv6 EVPN VXLAN 98

•

IBGP EVPN RRs/Route Reflectors are typically 8325, 8325H, 8360, 6400, 8400, or 9300 Switch Series.

•  Border VTEPs are typically 8360 Switch Series.

•  OSPFv3 is used to advertise and learn Loopbacks between VTEPs and RRs.

•

IPv6 BGP EVPN peering is established between Loopbacks on VTEPs and RRs.

•  VSX VTEPs utilize unique Lo0 for EVPN control plane and anycast Lo1 for data plane tunnel formation.

•  VSF VTEPs utilize Lo0 for EVPN control plane and data plane tunnel formation.

•  EVPN control plane - VTEPs do not need to peer with other VTEPs, they only need to peer with EVPN

RRs.

•  VXLAN data plane - full mesh VXLAN tunnels between VTEPs in a fabric are formed when they

interested in the same VNI/VXLAN Network Identifier.

lacp mode active

lag 1

no shutdown

ipv6 address link-local

ipv6 ospfv3 1 area 0.0.0.0

For the dual stack IPv4/IPv6 overlay with IPv6 unicast traffic forwarding:

•  Distributed L3 Gateways are deployed on VTEPs when the same VLAN needs to be extended across

•  Hosts can utilize static IPv6 IPs or SLAAC for dynamic IPv6 assignment.

•  The same link local fe80::1/64 address can be used, as the IPv6 default gateway on different VLANs/

SVIs as link local addresses are locally significant.

Use case— IPv6 EVPN VXLAN 101

! redistribute host-route is used to advertise host routes (/32 for

IPv4, /128 for IPv6)

! to remote VTEPs

! e.g. 10.1.10.100/32 and/or IPv6 address 2001:db8:1010::100/128 is

connected to VTEP1

! This is required as the same subnet is spread across multiple VTEPs

! Without host routes, a remote VTEP would not be aware of the correct

destination VTEP

! to send traffic

no shutdown

ip address 10.1.10.1/24

ipv6 address 2001:db8:1010::1/64

active-gateway ip 10.1.10.1

active-gateway ipv6 mac 12:00:00:00:01:00

interface vlan 11

vrf attach VRF1

ip address 10.1.11.1/24

ipv6 address 2001:db8:1011::1/64

active-gateway ip mac 12:00:00:00:01:00

active-gateway ip 10.1.11.1

active-gateway ipv6 mac 12:00:00:00:01:00

active-gateway ipv6 fe80::1
! SLAAC

no ipv6 nd suppress-ra dnssl

no ipv6 nd suppress-ra rdnss

! Allow RA, it is suppressed by default

ipv6 nd ra dns search-list local.net

ipv6 nd ra dns server fd25:bccf:1ffa:102::10

! Send RDNSS and DNSSL information

Public

Use case— IPv6 EVPN VXLAN 102

EVPN VSX support

NOTE

Not applicable to 6300 switch series, since 6300 supports VSF virtualization
technology instead of VSX.

VSX is a virtualization technology that presents the VSX pair as a single device to the layer 2 clients and as
independent devices at layer 3. The VSX pair provides an MCLAG link towards the clients. The clients see
the VSX-pair as a single switch, providing a redundant L2 connection to the clients. VSX acts in active-active
mode which also provides load-balancing to the connected clients.

EVPN is supported with VSX. The two VSX nodes act as independent BGP routing entities to the other
VTEPs or spines for control packets. However, in the datapath, both of them act as a single logical VTEP.
This is achieved by using different IP addresses for establishing the BGP session and using a common IP as
next-hop to represent the VTEP.

EVPN VSX support: Logical VTEP

Both the VSX pairs are configured with a common Logical VTEP. The configuration has to be done on a
loopback interface on both the VSX pairs. The underlay routing protocol (OSPF or even BGP) distributes this
logical VTEP IP address to all other VTEPs.

Public

EVPN VSX support 103

The Overlay routes are redistributed with the next-hop as the Logical VTEP IP address. The Overlay packets
reaching the Ingress VTEP get VXLAN encapsulated with the destination as the next-hop IP (common
Logical VTEP if the destination is the VSX pair) and sent to one of the Spines.

The Spine does ECMP on the VXLAN packet and sends it to either Leaf1 or Leaf1’ since both VSX switches
have connectivity to the common Logical VTEP.

Configuration recommendations for EVPN VSX support

•  The default Split Recovery setting (enabled) is the recommended configuration for VSX VTEPs.

•  All single homed hosts should be equally distributed across both VSX nodes.

•  The same Route Distinguisher (RD) are configured in both the VSX peers. The RD must be unique across

all the leafs except between VSX Peers.

•  Even in case of EBGP peering, both the VSX peers must be in the same AS.

•  The same VTEP IP must be configured on both the VSX peers.

•  The BGP source IP must be configured in both the VSX peers and it must be different from the logical

VTEP. This is to ensure that logical VTEP is not used as the source IP for the BGP session establishment
with the neighbor.

•  Routing session between VSX peers is recommended in case of upstream connectivity failure.

•  virtual-mac  is required for VSX distributed L3 gateways. The  virtual-mac  must be the

same as the VSX  system-mac  for a logical VTEP. The  virtual-mac  must be unique on each
VTEP.

•  VSX active forwarding must be enabled on the underlay VLAN to support MCLAG as an underlay for

EVPN VXLAN tunnels.

NOTE

VSX active-sync is not supported for the VXLAN interface. Ensure that the
configurations are manually configured between the VSX switches.
Overlay VLANs must be allowed on the ISL for MAC/ARP sync so both devices
can forward traffic.

vsx-sync  is not supported in EVPN feature-group.

For more details on VSX configurations, see the Virtual Switching Extension (VSX) Guide .

Subtopics

Sample configuration for iBGP VSX EVPN
VSX failure scenarios

Public

EVPN VSX support 104

Sample configuration for iBGP VSX EVPN

NOTE

The iBGP split-horizon rule is not followed between different address families.
Use route-map to block the routes getting advertised to the iBGP peer.

The following sample configuration solution shows the relevant EVPN/VSX configurations on the primary
and secondary VSX switches.

Spine configuration

interface loopback 1

    ip address 3.3.3.3/32

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

router bgp 1

    bgp router-id 3.3.3.3

    neighbor 2.2.2.2 remote-as 1

    neighbor 2.2.2.2 update-source 3.3.3.3

    neighbor 10.10.10.10 remote-as 1

    neighbor 10.10.10.10 update-source 3.3.3.3

    neighbor 20.20.20.20 remote-as 1

    neighbor 20.20.20.20 update-source 3.3.3.3

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 2.2.2.2 activate

        neighbor 2.2.2.2 route-reflector-client

        neighbor 2.2.2.2 send-community extended

        neighbor 10.10.10.10 activate

        neighbor 10.10.10.10 route-reflector-client

        neighbor 10.10.10.10 send-community extended

        neighbor 20.20.20.20 activate

        neighbor 20.20.20.20 route-reflector-client

        neighbor 20.20.20.20 send-community extended

    exit-address-family

Leaf1 configuration (Primary VTEP)

vlan 1-2

evpn

    vlan 2

        rd 1.1.1.1:2
        route-target export 2:2

        route-target import 2:2

interface 1/1/17

    no shutdown

Public

Sample configuration for iBGP VSX EVPN 105

no routing

    vlan trunk native 1 tag

    vlan trunk allowed all

interface loopback 1

description “vxlan source-ip”

    ip address 1.1.1.1/32

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

interface loopback 2

    ip address 10.10.10.10/32

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

interface vlan2

    ip address 10.1.1.5/24

    active-gateway ip mac 00:00:00:00:00:33

    active-gateway ip 10.1.1.10

vsx

    inter-switch-link 1/1/17

    role primary

    keepalive peer 20.1.1.2 source 20.1.1.1

interface vxlan 1

    source ip 1.1.1.1

    no shutdown

    vni 100

        vlan 2

    !

router bgp 1

    bgp router-id 10.10.10.10

    neighbor 3.3.3.3 remote-as 1

    neighbor 3.3.3.3 update-source 10.10.10.10

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 3.3.3.3 activate

        neighbor 3.3.3.3 send-community extended

    exit-address-family
vlan 1-2

evpn

    vlan 2

        rd 1.1.1.1:2

        route-target export 2:2

        route-target import 2:2

interface 1/1/17

    no shutdown

    no routing

    vlan trunk native 1 tag

    vlan trunk allowed all

description “vxlan source-ip”

    ip address 1.1.1.1/32

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

interface loopback 2

    ip address 10.10.10.10/32

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

interface vlan2

    ip address 10.1.1.5/24

    active-gateway ip mac 00:00:00:00:00:33

    active-gateway ip 10.1.1.10

vsx

interface 1/1/23

interface loopback 1

Public

Sample configuration for iBGP VSX EVPN 107

description “vxlan source-ip”

    ip address 1.1.1.1/32

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

interface loopback 2

    ip address 20.20.20.20/32

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

interface vlan2

    ip address 10.1.1.6/24

    active-gateway ip mac 00:00:00:00:00:33

    active-gateway ip 10.1.1.10

vsx

    inter-switch-link 1/1/23

    role secondary

    keepalive peer 20.1.1.1 source 20.1.1.2

interface vxlan 1

    source ip 1.1.1.1

    no shutdown

    vni 100

        vlan 2

    !

router bgp 1

    bgp router-id 20.20.20.20

    neighbor 3.3.3.3 remote-as 1

    neighbor 3.3.3.3 update-source 20.20.20.20

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 3.3.3.3 activate

        neighbor 3.3.3.3 send-community extended

    exit-address-family

Leaf2 configuration

vlan 1-2

evpn

    vlan 2

        rd 2.2.2.2:2

        route-target export 2:2

        route-target import 2:2

interface loopback 1

    ip address 2.2.2.2/32

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

interface vlan2

    ip address 10.1.1.7/24

    active-gateway ip mac 00:00:00:00:00:33

    active-gateway ip 10.1.1.10

    source ip 2.2.2.2

    no shutdown

    vni 100

        vlan 2

    !

router bgp 1

    neighbor 3.3.3.3 remote-as 1

    neighbor 3.3.3.3 update-source 2.2.2.2

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 3.3.3.3 activate

        neighbor 3.3.3.3 send-community extended

    exit-address-family

vlan 1-2

evpn

        rd 2.2.2.2:2

        route-target export 2:2

        route-target import 2:2

interface loopback 1

    ip address 2.2.2.2/32

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 3.3.3.3 activate

eBGP support for EVPN

The BGP session for underlay and overlay can be either iBGP or eBGP. eBGP EVPN is used for Data Center
use cases. Dual-AS and Multi-AS eBGP topologies are supported.

In a Dual-AS topology, all spines share a common AS number and all leafs share another common AS
number. For example:

In a Dual-AS topology, since the peer VTEPs (leafs) receive the route from another leaf with the same AS
number, the route would be rejected. To avoid this, use the following  allowas-in  command:

Public

eBGP support for EVPN 111

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 allowas-in 1
A sample Dual-AS configuration is shown below.

Leaf 1 configuration

vlan 1-2

evpn

    vlan 2

        rd 5:5

        route-target export 1:1

        route-target import 1:1

interface 1/1/1

    no shutdown

    ip address 11.1.1.2/24

interface loopback 1

    ip address 2.2.2.2/32

interface vxlan 1

    source ip 2.2.2.2

    no shutdown

    vni 100

interface vlan 2

router bgp 65501

    neighbor 1.1.1.1 remote-as 65501

    neighbor 1.1.1.1 update-source 2.2.2.2

    neighbor 11.1.1.1 remote-as 65501

    address-family ipv4 unicast

        neighbor 11.1.1.1 activate

        neighbor 11.1.1.1 allowas-in 1

        network 2.2.2.2/32

    exit-address-family

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 1.1.1.1 activate

        neighbor 1.1.1.1 allowas-in 1

        neighbor 1.1.1.1 send-community extended

    exit-address-family

Leaf 2 configuration

vlan 1-2

evpn
    vlan 2
        rd 5:5

        route-target export 1:1

        route-target import 1:1

interface 1/1/24

    no shutdown

interface vlan 2

    !

    neighbor 12.1.1.1 remote-as 65501

neighbor 12.1.1.1 remote-as 65501

        neighbor 12.1.1.1 activate

Spine configuration

vlan 1-2

interface 1/1/2

    no shutdown

    ip address 11.1.1.1/24

interface 1/1/5

    no shutdown

    ip address 12.1.1.1/24

interface loopback 1

router bgp 65500

    neighbor 2.2.2.2 remote-as 65500

    neighbor 2.2.2.2 update-source 1.1.1.1

    neighbor 3.3.3.3 remote-as 65500

    neighbor 3.3.3.3 update-source 1.1.1.1

    neighbor 11.1.1.2 remote-as 65500

        neighbor 11.1.1.2 activate

        neighbor 12.1.1.2 activate

        network 1.1.1.1/32

        neighbor 2.2.2.2 activate

        neighbor 2.2.2.2 next-hop-unchanged

        neighbor 2.2.2.2 send-community extended

        neighbor 3.3.3.3 next-hop-unchanged

        neighbor 3.3.3.3 send-community extended

    exit-address-family

vlan 1-2

interface 1/1/2

    ip address 11.1.1.1/24

In a Multi-AS Topology, all spines share a common AS number and all leafs have different AS numbers.

Figure 1. Multi-AS Topology

    no shutdown

Public

eBGP support for EVPN 117

ip address 11.1.1.1/24

interface 1/1/5

    no shutdown

    ip address 12.1.1.1/24

interface loopback 1

    ip address 1.1.1.1/32

router bgp 65501

    neighbor 2.2.2.2 remote-as 65501

    neighbor 2.2.2.2 update-source 1.1.1.1

    neighbor 3.3.3.3 remote-as 65502

    neighbor 3.3.3.3 update-source 1.1.1.1

    neighbor 11.1.1.2 remote-as 65503

    neighbor 12.1.1.2 remote-as 65504

    address-family ipv4 unicast

        neighbor 11.1.1.2 activate

        neighbor 12.1.1.2 activate

        network 1.1.1.1/32

    exit-address-family

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 2.2.2.2 activate

        neighbor 2.2.2.2 next-hop-unchanged

        neighbor 2.2.2.2 send-community extended

        neighbor 3.3.3.3 activate

        neighbor 3.3.3.3 next-hop-unchanged

        neighbor 3.3.3.3 send-community extended

    exit-address-family
The tunnel endpoints are communicated as next-hops in the EVPN routes. By default, the next hop of a
route is preserved when advertising the route to an iBGP peer, but is updated when advertising the route
to an eBGP peer. Setting this to 'true' over-rides this behavior and preserves the next hop when routes are
advertised to eBGP peer. To retain the next-hop (or tunnel endpoint info), an extra configuration is required
as shown in the following command:

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# neighbor 2.2.2.2 next-hop-unchanged

NOTE
When VSX is used with eBGP, the VSX peers must be in same AS.

Route-targets must be manually configured for switches running eBGP since
auto generated RT's (route-target auto) would lead to different export/import
route-targets (Applicable for both VSX/non-VSX).

MAC mobility

Public

MAC mobility 118

MAC mobility refers to the movement of a user terminal from one VTEP to another. The source VTEP is
unaware of the MAC move event. To notify other VTEPs of the change, the destination VTEP advertises a
MAC advertisement route. The source VTEP withdraws the old route for the MAC address after receiving
the new route. The MAC advertisement route has a sequence number that increases when the MAC address
moves. The sequence number identifies the most recent move if the MAC address moves multiple times.

EVPN MAC dampening

EVPN MAC dampening is used to detect duplicate MAC addresses in the network and provide a mechanism
to protect against endless MAC moves.

NOTE

EVPN MAC dampening is not supported on the 5420 or 6200 Switch Series.

As per RFC 7432, same MAC address is learned by different VTEPs in the same VLAN because two or more
hosts were configured incorrectly with the same MAC address. The traffic from the hosts triggers continuous
MAC moves among the VTEPs attached to the hosts. Every MAC mobility event for a given MAC address
will contain a sequence number of MAC routers received in route type-2. Based on this sequence number of
the MAC received and the MAC that is learnt locally, a decision to further withdraw or send a route update is
taken.

A VTEP receiving a MAC or IP advertisement route for a MAC address with a sequence number higher than
the one it had previously advertised, withdraws its MAC or IP advertisement route.

A VTEP detecting a locally attached MAC address for which it had previously received a MAC or IP
advertisement route, advertises the MAC address in a MAC or IP advertisement route. This advertisement
route is tagged with a MAC Mobility extended community attribute with a sequence number one greater
than the sequence number in the MAC Mobility extended community attribute of the received MAC or IP
advertisement route.

For the switch to recognize such instances and prevent the sequence number to increment infinitely, the
VTEP starts a timer when it detects a MAC mobility event involving a locally learnt MAC address. If the VTEP
detects a MAC move before the timer expires, it concludes that a duplicate MAC situation has occurred.
The VTEP alerts the operator through the event log and stops sending and processing any BGP MAC or IP
advertisement routes for the MAC address until the right action is provided.

NOTE
In a VSX setup, MAC dampening does not work as expected. Because, the VSX
primary dampens the MAC, but the VSX secondary will continue to advertise the
dampened MAC.

Following is the command syntax to configure EVPN MAC dampening:

mac-move-detection count <mac-move-count> timer <mac-move-timer>

Public

EVPN MAC dampening 119

active-gateway

Syntax

active-gateway {ip | ipv6} [<IP-ADDRESS>] [mac <MAC-ADDRESS>]

no active-gateway {ip | ipv6} [<IP-ADDRESS>] [mac]

Description

Configures an EVPN Anycast gateway that can be used on multiple VTEPs. The Active Gateway supports
both IPv4 and IPv6 addresses. The Active Gateway MAC address used along with an IPv4 and IPv6 address
must match on a given interface, for the EVPN Anycast Gateway solution to work as expected. Active
Gateway and SVI sharing the same IP is supported for both IPv4 and IPv6 addresses. The Active Gateway IP
for IPv6 should be a Link-Local IPv6 address if the default Gateway is learned via RA messages.

The no form of this command removes the active gateway for active-active routing.

Parameter

ip

ipv6

<IP‐ADDRESS>

Description

Specifies the configuration of an IPv4 address.

Specifies the configuration of an IPv6 address.

Specifies the IPv4 or IPv6 address.

•  Syntax for IPv4:  A.B.C.D
•  Syntax for IPv6:  A:B::C:D

Public

active-gateway 121

Parameter

Description

Specifies the Virtual MAC address. Syntax:  xx:xx:xx:xx:
xx:xx

<MAC‐ADDRESS>

Usage

Before configuring active gateway, confirm that an IP address is on the SVI that is in the same subnet as the
active gateway IP you are trying to configure. If an active gateway IP does not have an SVI IP with the same
subnet, the CLI allows the configuration, but the active gateway IP will not be programmed in the kernel,
resulting the active gateway to be unreachable.

Active forwarding cannot be configured when ICMP redirect is enabled. Enter the no ip icmp redirect
command for disabling ICMP redirect.

It is highly recommended that you use an IPv6 link-local address as a gateway (VIP) on the active gateway
IPv6 configuration.

If VRRP or active forwarding is configured on an SVI, active gateway cannot be configured. Active gateway
with overlapping networks is not allowed. Maximum of 16 unique virtual MACs are supported in a system.

The maximum number of supported active gateways per switch is 4,000. Since a maximum of 31 secondary
IPv4 addresses can be configured on an SVI, 32 IPv4 active gateways (along with the primary IPv4 address)
can be configured per SVI with IP multinetting support. This support is also the same for IPv6 addresses.

NOTE

When configuring a virtual active gateway, configure the active gateway IPv6
address before configuring an SVI IPv6 address, or Duplicate Address Detection
(DAD) may indicate that there are duplicate IP addresses.

NOTE

Do not use peer system MAC address as an active-gateway VMAC. If same MAC
address is used, the VSX synchronization will try to sync the configuration on
secondary switch and cause traffic disruptions.

Examples

Configuring active-gateway, showing the IP address is the same as the SVI IP address on both VSX peers
(valid for IPv4 and IPv6):

Switch 1:

switch1(config-if-vlan)# ip address 192.168.1.250/24

switch1(config-if-vlan)# active-gateway ip 192.168.1.250 mac

00:00:00:00:00:01

switch1(config-if-vlan)# active-gateway ipv6 fe80::100 mac 00:00:00:00:00:01

Public

active-gateway 122

switch1(config-if-vlan)# ipv6 address link-local fe80::100/64

Switch 2:

switch2(config-if-vlan)# ip address 192.168.1.250/24

switch2(config-if-vlan)# active-gateway ip 192.168.1.250 mac

00:00:00:00:00:01

switch2(config-if-vlan)# active-gateway ipv6 fe80::100 mac 00:00:00:00:00:01

switch2(config-if-vlan)# ipv6 address link-local fe80::100/64

Configuring only the active gateway address:

switch(config-if-vlan)# ip address 192.168.1.250/24

switch(config-if-vlan)# active-gateway ip 192.168.1.250

Configuring only the active gateway IP MAC address:

switch2(config-if-vlan)# ip address 192.168.1.250/24

switch2(config-if-vlan)# active-gateway ip mac 00:00:00:01:00:01

Removing the active gateway for active-active routing (IPv6 and IPv4):

switch(config-if-vlan)# no active-gateway ip

switch(config-if-vlan)# no active-gateway ipv6

Removing the active gateway for active-active routing for an IP address:

switch(config-if-vlan)# no active-gateway ip 192.168.1.250

Removing the active gateway for active-active routing for virtual MAC addresses:

switch(config-if-vlan)# no active-gateway ip mac

When configuring the virtual active gateway for IPv6 on an SVI, it is recommended to use the same global
IPv6 and active gateway IPv6 address. Similarly, if you want to use the IPv6 link-local address for the virtual
active gateway then the same address should be configured for both the SVI and the active gateway.

Global IPv6 address:

switch(config-if-vlan)# ipv6 address 1001::1/64

switch(config-if-vlan)# active-gateway ipv6 1001::1

switch(config-if-vlan)# active-gateway ipv6 mac 00:00:00:00:aa:01

IPv6-Link-Local address:

switch(config-if-vlan)# ipv6 address link-local fe80::1/64

switch(config-if-vlan)# active-gateway ipv6 fe80::1

switch(config-if-vlan)# active-gateway ipv6 mac 00:00:00:00:aa:01

Public

active-gateway 123

allow imet-relay

Public

allow imet-relay 124

Syntax

allow imet-relay

no allow imet-relay

Description

Allows the IMET routes to be relayed between EBGP peers.

The no form of this command blocks the IMET routes to be relayed between EBGP peers.

NOTE
Users must issue the clear bgp command after issuing the allow imet-relay / no
allow imet-relay command for the configuration to take effect.

Usage

Users are not encouraged to configure allow-imet-relay for the IMET routes to be relayed between EBGP
peers. Users are rather encouraged to use the set ip nexthop command in the route map context and
unchanged nexthop configurations to achieve the same functionality of allow-imet-relay.

Users are advised to use this command only in a single fabric deployment where the AOS-CX switches have
replaced a third party switch that was positioned as a router server relayed the IMET routes between EGBP
peers instead of terminating them.

This command should not be configured in any border VTEP and multifabric shared border deployments.
If configured on any switch, users may observe non-deterministic control-plane and data-plane behavior on
the switches.

Example

Allowing the IMET routes to be relayed between EBGP peers:

 switch(config)# evpn

 switch(config-evpn)# allow-imet-relay

allow imet-relay 125

arp-suppression

Syntax

arp-suppression

no arp-suppression

Description

Enables ARP suppression for EVPN-VxLAN globally. When enabled, switch will respond to the broadcast or
unicast ARP request if target address is present in the NDMD cache. Suppression is only supported for ARP
packets received on access ports. Suppression is not supported for ARP packets received on tunnel (VxLAN).
Disabled by default. Show running-config all will show the default ARP suppression values.

The no form of this command disables the ARP suppression.

NOTE

One the 8325 and 10000 Switch Series arp-suppression cannot be enabled with
arp-suppression-extended.

Examples

Configuring ARP suppression in EVPN:

switch(config-evpn)# arp-suppression

switch(config-evpn)# no arp-suppression

Public

arp-suppression 126

arp-suppression (enable | disable)

Syntax

arp-suppression {enable | disable}
no arp-suppression {enable | disable}

Public

arp-suppression (enable | disable) 127

Description

Enables ARP suppression for EVPN-VxLAN on specific VLAN. By default, ARP suppression for individual
VLANs is not enabled. Users must manually configure it to either enable or disable this feature.

The no form of this command reverts the VLAN to its default state, where ARP suppression is not active.

Parameter

Description

Enable ARP suppression for this VLAN.

<enable>

<disable>

Usage

Disable ARP suppression for this VLAN.

Suppression is only supported for ARP packets received on access ports.

Suppression is not supported for ARP packets received on tunnel (VxLAN).

ARP suppression for individual VLANs is supported for Active Gateway SVI IP addresses.

ARP suppression-extended feature is not available for individual VLANs.

1. Enabled globally, disabled at VLAN level:

If ARP suppression is enabled globally but explicitly disabled for a specific VLAN, the VLAN-specific
configuration takes precedence over the global setting. As a result, ARP suppression will not operate for the
VLAN where it has been explicitly disabled, even if it is enabled at the global level.

2. Disabled globally, enabled at VLAN level:

If ARP suppression is disabled globally but explicitly enabled for a specific VLAN, the VLAN-level
configuration overrides the global setting. ARP suppression will be active only for the VLANs where it
is explicitly enabled, regardless of the global configuration. All other VLANs will adhere to the global
configuration and have ARP suppression disabled.

Examples

Enabling ARP suppression on VLAN 10:

switch(config-evpn)# vlan 10
switch(config-evpn-vlan-10)# arp-suppression enable
switch(config-evpn-vlan-10)#

switch(config-evpn-vlan-10)# end

switch# show running-config evpn

evpn

Public

arp-suppression (enable | disable) 128

vlan 10

     arp-suppression enable

vlan 20

     arp-suppression disable

Removing ARP suppression on VLAN 10:

switch(config-evpn)# vlan 10

switch(config-evpn-vlan-10)# no arp-suppression enable

switch(config-evpn-vlan-10)# end

ARP suppression on VLAN 20:

switch(config-evpn-vlan-20)# no arp-suppression disable

switch(config-evpn-vlan-20)# end

switch# show running-config evpn

evpn

vlan 10

vlan 20

NOTE

For more information on features that use this command, refer to the Multicast
Guide for your switch model.

arp-suppression-extended

Syntax

arp-suppression-extended

no arp-suppression-extended

Description

Enables ARP suppression extension for EVPN-VxLAN globally. When enabled, the switch will respond to
the broadcast or unicast ARP request if target address is present in the NDMD cache. ARP suppression
extended cannot be enabled with ARP suppression. There is no separate command for enabling ARP
suppression only on L2 VLANs. The arp-suppression command enables ARP suppression on active
gateway SVIs. ARP suppression extended supports legacy ARP suppression functionality on active gateway
SVIs as well as ARP suppression on L2 VLANs. Migration to arp-suppression-extended is allowed only after
unconfiguring arp-suppression.

ARP suppression extended has the following requirements and limitations:

1. Suppression is disabled by default. The show running-config all command will show the default values.

2. Arp-suppression-extended cannot be enabled with arp-suppression. They are mutually-exclusive
commands.

3. There is no separate command for enabling ARP suppression only on L2 VLANs.

3. The arp-suppression command enables ARP suppression on active gateway SVIs.

4. The arp-suppression-extended command supports legacy ARP suppression functionality on active
gateway SVIs as well as ARP suppression on L2 VLANs

5. Migration to arp-suppression-extended is allowed only after unconfiguring arp-suppression. Only one
command can be configured at a time.

Public

arp-suppression-extended 130

6. On L2 VLAN to SVI conversion, the L2 Neighbors learned on pure L2 VLANs will be deleted. This will
lead to EVPN RT2 withdrawal for these neighbors on all the remote VTEPs. Local host MAC-IP on SVI will be
relearned after the traffic arrives.

7. When arp-suppression-extended is disabled, the dynamic MAC-IP bindings on L2 VLANs will be deleted.
This will lead to EVPN RT2 withdrawal for these neighbors on all remote VTEPs.

8. Suppression is only supported for ARP packets received on access ports.

9. Suppression is not supported for ARP packets received on tunnel (VxLAN).

The no form of this command disables ARP suppression extension.

NOTE

One the 6300, 6400, 8100, 8325, 8360, 9300, 9300S, 10000, and 10040 Switch
Series arp-suppression cannot be enabled with arp-suppression-extended.

Examples

Configuring ARP suppression extended:

switch(config-evpn)# arp-suppression-extended

Configuring ARP suppression extended when ARP suppression has already been configured:

switch(config-evpn)# arp-suppression

switch(config-evpn)# arp-suppression-extended

arp-suppression-extended and arp-suppression cannot be configured together.

arp-suppression-extended

Syntax

arp-suppression-extended {enable|disable}

no arp-suppression-extended {enable|disable}

Description

Enables ARP suppression extended for EVPN-VxLAN on a specific VLAN. When enabled, the switch
responds to the broadcast or unicast ARP request if target address is present in the neighbor cache.

The no form of this command removes this configuration.

Parameter

{enable|disable}

Usage

Description

Enables or disables arp suppression extended for th
e specified VLAN.

Suppression is only supported for ARP packets received on access ports. Suppression is not supported for
ARP packets received on tunnel (VxLAN). ARP Suppression Extended per VLAN is not set by default. It must
configured in order to enable or disable. Per VLAN arp-suppression-extended is mutually exclusive with
global and per vlan arp-suppression. ARP Suppression Extended on per VLAN is supported for both Active
Gateway SVIs IP and L2 VLANs. All existing ARP suppression extended guidelines are applicable to ARP
suppression extended on a per-VLAN basis.

Per VLAN ARP Suppression Extended takes more precedence than Global ARP Suppression Extended. When
ARP suppression extended is enabled globally but disabled for a specific VLAN, the global configuration is
overridden for that VLAN. ARP suppression extended does not function for the VLAN where it is explicitly

Public

arp-suppression-extended 132

disabled, even if it is enabled globally. Other VLANs still follow the global configuration and have ARP
suppression extended enabled.

When ARP suppression extended is disabled globally but enabled for a specific VLAN, the VLAN-level
configuration takes precedence. ARP suppression extended functions only for the VLANs where it is
explicitly enabled, ignoring the global configuration. Other VLANs follow the global configuration and have
ARP suppression extended disabled.

Examples

Enabling or disabling ARP suppression extended for EVPN-VxLAN on VLAN 10:

switch(config-evpn-vlan-10)# arp-suppression-extended

disable  Disable ARP suppression extended for this VLAN.

enable   Enable ARP suppression extended for this VLAN.

arp-suppression-extended 133

arp-suppression-extended exclude

Syntax

arp-suppression-extended exclude {IP-ADDR}

no arp-suppression-extended exclude {IP-ADDR}

Description

Exempts certain IP addresses from ARP suppression extended and allows ARP requests for those IP
addresses to be flooded across the VXLAN fabric even though the target addresses are present in the
NDMD cache.

For certain IP addresses, it may be necessary to allow ARP requests to be forwarded across the network
even when suppression extended is enabled.

Maximum IP addresses can be exempted from ARP Suppression Extended per VLAN are 256.

Maximum IP addresses can be exempted from ARP Suppression Extended across all VLANs are 256.

The no form of this command removes this configuration.

Parameter

<IP-ADDR>

Examples

Description

Specifies the IP address.

Exempting certain IP addresses from ARP suppression extended on VLAN 10:

switch(config-evpn-vlan-10)# arp-suppression-extended exclude 10.1.1.1
Removing the exemption for certain IP addresses from ARP suppression extended on VLAN 10:

switch(config-evpn-vlan-10)# no arp-suppression-extended exclude 10.1.1.1

dyn-vxlan-tunnel-bridging-mode

Syntax

dyn-vxlan-tunnel-bridging-mode ibgp-ebgp

no dyn-vxlan-tunnel-bridging-mode ibgp-ebgp

Description

Specifies the dyn-vxlan-tunnel-bridging-mode and provides the configurations to enable bridging between
dynamic tunnels (BGP-EVPN-provisioned VXLAN tunnels). By default, split-horizon is enabled for all
dynamic tunnels; therefore, bridging is disabled between them.

The default value is NULL.

The no form of this command disables bridging between dynamic tunnels.

Usage

•  Configuration of static tunnels is not recommended on VTEPs where iBGP-eBGP split horizon is

disabled.

•

iBGP and eBGP tunnels are part of different broadcast groups and bridging is permitted between them.

Public

dyn-vxlan-tunnel-bridging-mode 137

•  Route Targets (RTs) have to be manually configured by the user.

Examples

Enabling bridging between dynamic tunnels:

switch(config-evpn)#dyn-vxlan-tunnel-bridging-mode ibgp-ebgp

Disabling bridging between dynamic tunnels:

switch(config-evpn)# no dyn-vxlan-tunnel-bridging-mode ibgp-ebgp

evpn ethernet segment

Syntax

evpn-ethernet-segment esi <ESI-TYPE> {ESI|AUTO}

no evpn-ethernet-segment esi <ESI-TYPE> {ESI|AUTO}

Description

Configures EVPN multihoming ESI (Ethernet Segment Identifier) in a 00XX.XXXX.XXXX.XXXX.XXXX format
at the interface context. Consider that:

•  ESI configuration is allowed on L2 interfaces only.

•  EVPN multihoming and VSX are mutually exclusive. ESI cannot be configured on ISL and MCLAG

interfaces.

Public

evpn ethernet segment 143

•  Only Type-0 and Type-1 ESIs are supported with this release.

The no form of this command removes this configuration.

Parameter

ESI

<ESI-TYPE>

AUTO

Usage

Description

Ethernet Segment Identifier. Syntax: 00XX.XXXX.XX
XX.XXXX.XXXX

Specifies the ESI type. Only Type-0 and Type-1 are s
upported.

Uses the auto ESI.

Unconfiguring ESI of a ethernet-segment via CLI internally removes RD and import route-target of the
ethernet-segment.

If LEAF1 and LEAF2 are ES members, when LEAF1 learns a dynamic MAC, it sends route type-2 for MAC
advertisement. When LEAF2 receives this type-2, it installs evpn-local MAC on the ES-port. Similarly, when
LEAF1 learns a dynamic neighbor, it sends route type-2 for MAC-IP advertisement. When LEAF2 receives
this type-2, it installs evpn-local neighbor on the ES-port.

If evpn-local MAC is already present and dynamic MAC is learnt or vice versa, dynamic learning always
takes precedence. Furthermore, evpn-local MAC never ages out even though it points to an access port. It's
book-keeping is driven via BGP-EVPN control plane.

With multihoming enabled, when a mac-mobility event is detected, the ESI value is considered for
incrementing mac-mobility sequence number. This means that, if A PE learns a locally attached MAC
(or Neighbor) for which it had previously received a MAC/IP Advertisement route with the same ESI, it is
not considered as mac-move and as a result, the mac-mobility sequence number is not incremented while
advertising type-2 route for the MAC/IP learn. Similarly, if A PE has learnt a local attached MAC address on
an ethernet-segment port, and then if it receives a MAC/IP Advertisement route with the same ESI, it is not
considered as mac-move.

Examples

Configuring EVPN multihoming ESI in an interface:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# no routing

switch(config-if)# evpn-ethernet-segment esi type-0 0011.2222.3333.4444.5555

switch(config-if)# no evpn-ethernet-segment esi type-0

0011.2222.3333.4444.5555
switch(config-if)# exit
Configuring EVPN multihoming ESI for type-1:

Public

evpn ethernet segment 144

switch(config)# interface lag 1

switch(config-lag-if)# no routing

switch(config-lag-if)# evpn-ethernet-segment esi type-1 auto

switch(config-if)# no evpn-ethernet-segment esi type-1 auto

switch(config-if)# exit
Configuring EVPN multihoming ESI in an interface for type-1:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# no routing

switch(config-if)# evpn-ethernet-segment esi type-1 auto

switch(config-if)# no evpn-ethernet-segment esi type-1 auto

switch(config-if)# exit

evpn ethernet segment import rt

Syntax

evpn-ethernet-segment import-route-target {RT}

no evpn-ethernet-segment import-route-target {RT}

Description

Configures the import route target for EVPN multihoming ethernet segment at the interface context.

The no form of this command removes this configuration.

Public

evpn ethernet segment import rt 145

Parameter

{RT}

Usage

Description

Specifies the route target.

Import route target configuration for ethernet sement is allowed only after the ESI is configured. Only type-6
RTs can be configured for ethernet segment.

The no form of this command removes this configuration.

Examples

Configuring the import route target for EVPN multihoming ethernet segment in an interface:

switch(config)# interface lag 1

switch(config-lag-if)# no routing

switch(config-lag-if)# evpn-ethernet-segment esi type-0

0011.2222.3333.4444.5555

switch(config-lag-if)# evpn-ethernet-segment import-route-target

1111:2222:3333

switch(config-lag-if)# exit
Removing the import route target for EVPN multihoming ethernet segment in an interface:

switch(config)# interface lag 1

switch(config-lag-if)# no routing

switch(config-lag-if)# evpn-ethernet-segment esi type-0

0011.2222.3333.4444.5555

switch(config-lag-if)# no evpn-ethernet-segment import-route-target

1111:2222:3333

switch(config-if)# shutdown

switch(config-if)# no evpn-ethernet-segment esi type-0

0011.2222.3333.4444.5555

switch(config-lag-if)# exit

config‐if
config‐lag-if

ts for this command.

igmp-mld-proxy

Syntax

igmp-mld-proxy

no igmp-mld-proxy

Description

Enables optimized Layer 2 multicast by using SMET routes (EVPN Route Type 6). When enabled alongside
IGMP or MLD snooping, SMET routes are advertised instead of IGMP or MLD messages, enhancing multicast
efficiency. Additionally, this command triggers an IMET route update for all VNIs with the multicast flags
extended community, setting the IGMP and MLD proxy bits. IGMP-MLD proxy is disabled by default.

The no form of this command removes the configuration of IGMP-MLD Proxy using EVPN Route type 6.

NOTE
This command is supported with Underlay Multicast replication in PIM-BIDIR,
PIM-BIDIR Transit, and PIM-SM modes.

NOTE
The replication-mode underlay-multicast advertise command must be
disabled on the 6300, 6400, and 8360 Switch series.

Public

igmp-mld-proxy 148

Usage

Enabling this command switches the Layer 2 multicast operating mode from CX's native mode (using
IGMP/MLD messages) to standards-based SMET routes within the fabric. IGMP/MLD snooping must be
configured on all participating VTEPs in the multicast network. This command also automatically starts the
multicast Querier functionality on all VNI-mapped VLANs, using the SVI IP if configured, or the globally
configured Querier IP (ip igmp snooping querier-address) if no SVI is present. Additionally, enabling this
sets the multicast flag extended community in the IMET route, triggering a tunnel reconfiguration that may
briefly impact traffic.

NOTE

This feature does not support multihoming (using Route Type 7 and 8.)

Examples

Configuring IGMP-MLD Proxy using EVPN Route type 6.

switch(config-evpn)# igmp-mld-proxy

Removing the configuration of IGMP-MLD Proxy using EVPN Route type 6.

switch(config-evpn)# no igmp-mld-proxy

st.

Command introduced.

igmp-mld-proxy forward-to-non-proxy

Syntax

igmp-mld-proxy forward-to-non-proxy

no igmp-mld-proxy forward-to-non-proxy

Description

Enables the switch to always forward multicast traffic to the set of VTEPs that do not support IGMP or
MLD proxies (RFC 9251). This includes any VTEP that has advertised an Inclusive Multicast Ethernet
Tag (IMET) route for the BD without a Multicast Flags Extended Community. By default, igmp-mld-proxy
forward-to-non-proxy is disabled.

The no form of this command disables the configuration.

Usage

In a fabric with a mix of VTEP's that support IGMP-MLD Proxy and those that do not, this command allows
the switch to forward multicast traffic to legacy devices that lack IGMP-MLD Proxy support. Normally, with
snooping enabled, VLAN traffic does not reach these legacy devices. Enabling this feature ensures that
legacy devices receive the traffic needed to serve it's attached receivers.

Enabling this on a fabric with a large number of legacy devices causes proxy-capable devices to forward
multicast traffic to all legacy VTEPs, potentially impacting performance.

NOTE
This configuration should only be used when there are non-IGMP/MLD Proxy-
capable devices in the network with attached receivers.

Public

igmp-mld-proxy forward-to-non-proxy 150

This command is effective only when used in conjunction with IGMP/MLD Proxy and IGMP/MLD snooping.
By default, traffic is not forwarded to non-proxy-capable devices unless these settings are enabled.

NOTE

•  This command is not supported when using Underlay Multicast as the

replication mode.

•  Hybrid deployments of proxy-capable and non-proxy-capable VTEPs are not
supported in Underlay Multicast mode. All nodes in the fabric must have
IGMP/MLD proxy enabled.

Examples

Enabling the switch to always forward multicast traffic to the set of VTEPs that do not support IGMP or MLD
proxies.

switch(config-evpn)# igmp-mld-proxy forward-to-non-proxy

Removing the configuration of always forwarding multicast traffic to the set of VTEPs that do not support
IGMP or MLD proxies.

switch(config-evpn)# no igmp-mld-proxy forward-to-non-proxy

mac-move-detection count timer

Syntax

mac-move-detection count <MAC-MOVE-COUNT> timer <MAC-MOVE-TIMER>

no mac-move-detection count <MAC-MOVE-COUNT> timer <MAC-MOVE-TIMER>

Description

Configures EVPN MAC dampening for duplicate MAC and MAC-move count and timer across VTEPs.

The no form of this command resets the value of the count and timer to the default values of  5  and  180
seconds respectively.

NOTE

EVPN MAC dampening is always enabled. Links to the VTEPs must be always up
for EVPN MAC dampening to be activated.

Parameter

Description

count <MAC‐MOVE‐COUNT>

Specifies the number of MAC‐moves for MAC dampening to
take effect. Range: 2 to 10. Default: 5.

timer <MAC‐MOVE‐TIMER>

Specifies the MAC‐move time limit in seconds for MAC dampe
ning to take effect. Range: 1 to 1000 seconds. Default: 180 sec
onds.

Examples

Configuring EVPN MAC dampening:

Public

mac-move-detection count timer 152

switch(config-evpn)# mac-move-detection count 6 timer 199

The above command dampens a MAC if the MAC moves six times within 199 seconds.

switch(config-evpn)# mac-move-detection count 8

The above command dampens a MAC if the MAC moves eight times within 180 seconds.

switch(config-evpn)# mac-move-detection timer 255

The above command dampens a MAC if the MAC moves five times within 255 seconds.

multihoming system-id

Syntax

multihoming system-id <MAC-ADDR>

no multihoming system-id <MAC-ADDR>

Description

Configures the actor's system-id to be used by LACP in EVPN multihoming deployments.

The no form of this command removes this configuration.

Parameter

<MAC-ADDR>

Usage

Description

Specifies the MAC address.

By default, LACP uses system-mac as the actor's system-id. In VSX deployments, the default behavior can
be overriden by system-mac under the VSX context. However, VSX and EVPN multihoming are mutually
exclusive. Therefore, in EVPN multihoming deployments, a custom system-id can be configured using
multihoming-system-id under the EVPN context.

Examples

Configuring EVPN multihoming system-id:

switch(config-evpn)# multihoming-system-id 00:11:22:33:44:55
Removing EVPN multihoming system-id:

switch(config-evpn)# no multihoming-system-id 00:11:22:33:44:55

nd-suppression

Syntax

nd-suppression

no nd-suppression

Description

Enables IPv6 nd suppression globally for EVPN-VxLAN. When enabled, the switch will respond to the IPv6
multicast or unicast neighbor solicitation if target address is present in the NDMD cache. Suppression is only
supported for NS packets received on access ports. Suppression is not supported for NS packets received on
tunnel (VxLAN). Disabled by default. Show running-config all will show the default ND suppression values.

The no form of this command disables ND suppression.

NOTE
One the 8325 and 10000 Switch Series nd-suppression cannot be enabled with
nd-suppression-extended.

Examples

Configuring ND suppression in EVPN:

switch(config-evpn)# nd-suppression

switch(config-evpn)# no nd-suppression

nd-suppression (enable | disable)

Syntax

nd-suppression {enable | disable}

no nd-suppression {enable | disable}

Description

Enables ND suppression for EVPN-VxLAN on specific VLAN. When enabled, the switch will reply to IPv6
multicast or unicast neighbor solicitation messages if the target address is found in the NDMD cache.

The no form of this command resets ND suppression to default for EVPN-VxLAN on specific VLAN.

Public

nd-suppression (enable | disable) 156

Parameter

Description

Enable ND suppression for this VLAN.

<enable>

<disable>

Usage

Disable ND suppression for this VLAN.

Suppression is only supported for NS packets received on access ports.

Suppression is not supported for ND packets received on tunnel (VxLAN).

Per-VLAN suppression is not enabled by default. Users must manually configure it to enable or disable the
feature.

Per-VLAN ND suppression is mutually exclusive with ND suppression extended.

ND suppression on a per-VLAN basis is supported for Active Gateway SVI IP addresses. However, per-VLAN
ND suppression extended is not supported.

1. Enabled globally, disabled at VLAN level:

When ND suppression is enabled globally but explicitly disabled for a specific VLAN, the VLAN-specific
configuration takes precedence over the global setting.

ND suppression will not operate for the VLAN where it is explicitly disabled, even if it is enabled globally. All
other VLANs will adhere to the global configuration and have ND suppression enabled.

2. Disabled globally, enabled at VLAN level:

When ND suppression is disabled globally but explicitly enabled for a specific VLAN, the VLAN-level
configuration takes precedence over the global setting.

ND suppression will be active only for VLANs where it is explicitly enabled, overriding the global
configuration. All other VLANs will follow the global configuration and have ND suppression disabled.

Examples

Enabling ND suppression on VLAN 10:

switch(config-evpn)# vlan 10
switch(config-evpn-vlan-10)# nd-suppression enable
switch(config-evpn-vlan-10)#

switch(config-evpn-vlan-10)# end

switch# show running-config evpn

evpn

Public

nd-suppression (enable | disable) 157

vlan 10

     nd-suppression enable

vlan 20

     nd-suppression disable

ND suppression on VLAN 10:

switch(config-evpn)# vlan 10

switch(config-evpn-vlan-10)# no nd-suppression enable

switch(config-evpn-vlan-10)# end

ND suppression on VLAN 20:

switch(config-evpn-vlan-20)# no nd-suppression disable

switch(config-evpn-vlan-20)# end

switch# show running-config evpn

evpn

vlan 10

vlan 20

NOTE

For more information on features that use this command, refer to the Multicast
Guide for your switch model.

nd-suppression-extended

Syntax

nd-suppression-extended

no nd-suppression-extended

Description

Enables IPv6 ND suppression extension globally for EVPN-VxLAN. When enabled, switch will respond to the
IPv6 multicast or unicast neighbor solicitation if target address is present in the NDMD cache. Disabled by
default.

ND suppression extension has the following requirements and limitations:

1. Suppression is disabled by default. The show running-config all command will show the default values.

2. The nd-suppression-extended command cannot be used with the nd-suppression command.

3. There is no separate command for enabling ND suppression by itself on L2 VLANs.

4. The nd-suppression command enables ND suppression on active gateway SVIs.

5. The nd-suppression-extended command supports ND suppression functionality on active gateway SVIs
as well as ND suppression on L2 VLANs.

6. On L2 VLAN to SVI conversion, the L2 Neighbors learned on pure L2 VLANs will be deleted. This will
lead to EVPN RT2 withdrawal for these neighbors on all remote VTEPs. Local host MAC-IP on SVI will be
relearned after the traffic arrives.

7. When nd-suppression-extended is disabled, the dynamic MAC-IP bindings on L2 VLANs will be deleted.
This will lead to EVPN RT2 withdrawal for these neighbors on all remote VTEPs.

8. Suppression is only supported for NS packets received on access ports.

9. Suppression is not supported for ND packets received on tunnel (VxLAN).

Public

nd-suppression-extended 159

The no form of this command disables ND suppression extension.

NOTE

One the 6300, 6400, 8100, 8325, 8360, 9300, 9300S, 10000, and 10040 Switch
Series nd-suppression cannot be enabled with nd-suppression-extended.

Examples

Configuring ND suppression extended:

switch(config-evpn)# nd-suppression-extended

Configuring ND suppression extended when ND suppression has already been configured:

switch(config-evpn)# nd-suppression

switch(config-evpn)# nd-suppression-extended

nd-suppression-extended and arp-suppression cannot be configured together.

nd-suppression-extended

Syntax

nd-suppression-extended {enable|disable}

no nd-suppression-extended {enable|disable}

Description

Enables ARP suppression extended for EVPN-VxLAN on a specific VLAN. When enabled, the switch
responds to the IPv6 multicast or unicast neighbor solicitation if target address is present in the NDMD
cache.

The no form of this command removes this configuration.

Parameter

{enable|disable}

Usage

Description

Enables or disables nd suppression extended for the
specified VLAN.

Suppression is only supported for NS packets received on access ports. Suppression is not supported for ND
packets received on tunnel (VxLAN). ND Suppression Extended on per VLAN is not set by default. It must
configured in order to enable or disable.

Per VLAN ND Suppression Extended is mutually exclusive with global and per vlan nd-suppression. ND
Suppression Extended on per VLAN is supported for both Active Gateway SVIs IP and L2 VLANs. All
existing ND suppression extended guidelines are applicable to ND suppression extended on a per-VLAN
basis.

Per VLAN ND Suppression Extended takes more precedence than Global ND Suppression Extended.

When ND suppression extended is enabled globally but disabled for a specific VLAN, the global
configuration is overridden for that VLAN. ND suppression extended does not function for the VLAN where
it is explicitly disabled, even though it is enabled globally. Other VLANs still follow the global configuration
and have ND suppression extended enabled.

When ND suppression extended is disabled globally but enabled for a specific VLAN, the VLAN-level
configuration takes precedence. ND suppression extended functions only for the VLANs where it is explicitly
enabled, ignoring the global configuration. Other VLANs follow the global configuration and have ND
suppression extended disabled.

Public

nd-suppression-extended 161

Examples

Enabling or disabling ARP suppression extended for EVPN-VxLAN on VLAN 10:

switch(config-evpn-vlan-10)# nd-suppression-extended

disable  Disable ARP suppression extended for this VLAN.

enable   Enable ARP suppression extended for this VLAN.

nd-suppression-extended exclude

Public

nd-suppression-extended exclude 162

Syntax

nd-suppression-extended exclude {IPV6-ADDR}

no nd-suppression-extended exclude {IPV6-ADDR}

Description

Exempts certain IPv6 addresses from ND suppression extended and allows neighbor solicitation (NS) for
those IPv6 addresses to be flooded across the VXLAN fabric even though the target addresses are present
in the NDMD cache.

For certain IPv6 addresses, it may be necessary to allow NS to be forwarded across the network even when
suppression extended is enabled.

Maximum IPv6 addresses can be exempted from ND Suppression Extended per VLAN are 256.

Maximum IPv6 addresses can be exempted from ND Suppression Extended across all VLANs are 256.

The no form of this command removes this configuration.

Parameter

<IPV6-ADDR>

Examples

Description

Specifies the IPv6 address.

Exempting certain IPv6 addresses from ND suppression extended on VLAN 10:

switch(config-evpn-vlan-10)# nd-suppression-extended exclude 100::1
Removing the exemption for certain IPv6 addresses from ND suppression extended on VLAN 10:

switch(config-evpn-vlan-10)# no nd-suppression-extended exclude 100::1

Description

This command exempts certain IPv6 addresses from ND suppression and allows neighbor solicitation (NS)
for those IPv6 addresses to be flooded across the VXLAN fabric even though the target addresses are
present in the NDMD cache. For certain IPv6 addresses, it may be necessary to allow NS to be forwarded

The no form of this command disables the IP exemption.

is 256.

rd

Syntax

rd {auto | <AS-NUMBER:ID> | <IP-ADDRESS:ID>}

no rd {auto | <AS-NUMBER:ID> | <IP-ADDRESS:ID>}

Description

Specifies a unique number prepended to the advertised routes within the VLAN. It ensures support for
overlapping IP addresses and MACs across different tenants. The default value is NULL. Route Distinguisher
(RD) has to be manually configured by a user.

The no form of this command removes the currently configured value.

Parameter

auto

Description

Specifies automatic route filtering.

<AS‐NUMBER:ID>

<IP‐ADDRESS:ID>

Specifies the AS number. It can be a 1‐byte or 4‐byte value.
If the AS number is a 2‐byte value, the administrative number
is a 4‐byte value and if the AS number is 4‐byte value, the a
dministrative number is a 2‐byte value.

Specifies the IP address. It is a 4‐byte value and the ID is 2 by
tes.

Examples

Configuring Route Distinguisher for EVPN VLAN:

switch(config-evpn)# vlan 10

switch(config-evpn-vlan-10)# rd 6800:1

switch(config-evpn)# vlan 20
switch(config-evpn-vlan-20)# rd auto

Public

rd 166

Syntax

rd {<AS-NUMBER:NN> | <IP-ADDRESS:NN>}

Public

rd (evpn vlan-aware-bundles) 167

Description

Specifies a unique number prepended to the EVPN routes, advertised in the context of any VLAN configured
under an EVPN VLAN aware bundle instance.

The no form of this command removes this configuration.

Parameter

Description

Specifies the AS number. The AS number can be a 2‐byte or
4‐byte value. If the AS number is a 2‐byte value, the admin
istrative number is a 4‐byte value and if the AS number is 4
‐byte value, the administrative number is a 2‐byte value.

Specifies the IP address. The IP address is a 4‐byte value and
the ID is a 2‐‐byte value.

<AS‐NUMBER:NN>

<IP‐ADDRESS:NN>

Usage

This command ensures the support for overlapping IPs and MACs across different tenants.  rd  should be
manually configured.  rd  should be unique acorss all VLAN-based (VLANs) and VLAN-aware (bundles)
service instances.

Examples

Configuring rd 6800:1.

switch(config-evpn)# vlan-aware-bundle bundle_1

switch(config-evpn-vlan-aware-bundle-bundle_1)# rd 6800:1

Removing rd 6800:1.

switch(config-evpn)# vlan-aware-bundle bundle_1

switch(config-evpn-vlan-aware-bundle-bundle_1)# no rd 6800:1

Configuring rd 1.2.3.4:55.

switch(config-evpn)# vlan-aware-bundle bundle_1

switch(config-evpn-vlan-aware-bundle-bundle_1)# rd 1.2.3.4:55

Removing rd 1.2.3.4:55.

switch(config-evpn)# vlan-aware-bundle bundle_1
switch(config-evpn-vlan-aware-bundle-bundle_1)# no rd 1.2.3.4:55

Platforms

Command context

Authority

All platforms

config‐evpn

Administrators or local user group members with execution righ
ts for this command.

redistribute local-svi

Syntax

redistribute local-svi

no redistribute local-svi

Description

Enables type-2 route advertisement for the local IP address and MAC address of the SVI interfaces
corresponding to the EVPN-enabled VLANs.

The no form of this command disables type-2 route advertisement for the local IP address and MAC address
of the SVI interfaces corresponding to the EVPN-enabled VLANs.

Examples

Enabling type-2 route advertisement:

switch(config)# evpn

switch(config-evpn)# redistribute local-svi

Disabling type-2 route advertisement:

switch(config)# evpn

switch(config-evpn)# no redistribute local-svi

Public

redistribute local-svi 173

Modification

10.07 or earlier

route-target

Syntax

route-target {import | export | both} {auto | <AS-NUMBER:ID> | <IP-

ADDRESS:ID>}

no route-target {import | export | both} {auto | <AS-NUMBER:ID> | <IP-

ADDRESS:ID>}

Public

route-target 174

Description

Controls the import and export of VPN routes only to the systems in the network for which routes are
needed. The default value is NULL. Route Targets (RT) have to be manually configured by a user.

The no form of this command removes the currently configured value.

Parameter

import

export

both

auto

<AS‐NUMBER:ID>

<IP‐ADDRESS:ID>

Description

Configures the route‐target to import EVPN routes.

Configures the route‐target to export EVPN routes.

Configures the route‐target to import and export EVPN rou
tes.

Specifies automatic route filtering.

Specifies the AS number. It can be a 1‐byte or 4‐byte value.
If the AS number is a 2‐byte value, the administrative number
is a 4‐byte value and if the AS number is 4‐byte value, the a
dministrative number is a 2‐byte value.

Specifies the IP address. It is a 4‐byte value and the ID is 2 by
tes.

Examples

Configuring Route Targets for EVPN VLAN:

switch(config-evpn)# vlan 10

switch(config-evpn-vlan-10)# route-target import 6800:1

switch(config-evpn-vlan-10)# route-target export 6800:1

switch(config-evpn)# vlan 20

switch(config-evpn-vlan-20)# route-target both 6900:1

Command History

Release

Modification

10.07 or earlier

‐‐

route-target auto mode

Syntax

route-target auto mode legacy

no route-target auto mode legacy

Description

Configures format alternatives for auto-generation of route-targets. Mode legacy indicates that the route
targets are auto-generated based on RFC4364 which is in ASN:VNI format. By default, route-targets are
auto-generated based on RFC8365. Only 2-byte ASN value auto-generation is supported in this version of
AOS-CX. For 4-byte ASN, value of 23456(AS_TRANS) will be used in place of ASN followed by VNI.

The no form of this command removes the currently configured value.

Public

route-target auto mode 176

Parameter

legacy

Examples

Description

Configures to auto‐generate route‐target in ASN:VNI format.
This parameter is required.

Configuring alternative route-targets for EVPN VLAN:

switch(config-evpn)# vlan 10

switch(config-evpn-vlan-10)# route-target auto mode legacy

route-target auto mode 177

route-target {evpn}

Syntax

route-target {import | export | both} {auto | <AS-NUMBER:ID> | <IP-

ADDRESS:ID>} evpn

no route-target {import | export | both} {auto | <AS-NUMBER:ID> | <IP-

ADDRESS:ID>} evpn

Description

Configures the route target (RT) for EVPN VRF to control the import and export of VPN routes only to
the systems in the network for which routes are needed. The default value is NULL. Route targets have to
be manually configured by a user. Route-targets will be auto-generated in ASN:VNI format when 'auto' is
configured. For 4-byte ASN, value of 23456(AS_TRANS) will be used in place of ASN followed by VNI.

The no form of this command removes the RT in EVPN VRF.

Parameter

Description

import

export

both

auto

<AS‐NUMBER:ID>

<IP‐ADDRESS:ID>

Imports the VRF routes that match the RT.

Exports the RT in the VRF routes.

Configures both import and export of routes for the VRF.

Specifies automatic route filtering.

Specifies the AS number. It can be a 1‐byte or 4‐byte value.
If the AS number is a 2‐byte value, the administrative number
is a 4‐byte value and if the AS number is 4‐byte value, the a
dministrative number is a 2‐byte value.

Specifies the IP address. It is a 4‐byte value and the ID is 2 by
tes.

Examples

Configuring Route Targets for EVPN VRF:

switch(config-vrf)# route-target import 6800:1 evpn

switch(config-vrf)# route-target export 6800:1 evpn

switch(config-vrf)# route-target both 6800:1 evpn

Public

route-target {evpn} 178

switch(config-vrf)# route-target import auto evpn

switch(config-vrf)# route-target export auto evpn

switch(config-vrf)# route-target both auto evpn

route-target (evpn vlan-aware-bundles)

Public

route-target (evpn vlan-aware-bundles) 179

Syntax

route-target {import | export | both} {<AS-NUMBER:ID> | <IP-ADDRESS:ID>}

no route-target {import | export | both} {<AS-NUMBER:ID> | <IP-ADDRESS:ID>}

Description

Enables the import and export of EVPN routes only to the systems in the network for which routes are
needed.

The no form of this command removes this configuration.

Description

Configures the route‐target to import EVPN routes.

Configures the route‐target to export EVPN routes.

Configures the route‐target to import and export EVPN rou
tes.

Specifies the AS number. It can be a 2‐byte or 4‐byte value.
If the AS number is a 2‐byte value, the administrative number
is a 4‐byte value and if the AS number is 4‐byte value, the a
dministrative number is a 2‐byte value.

Specifies the IP address. It is a 4‐byte value and the ID is 2 by
tes.

Parameter

import

export

both

<AS‐NUMBER:ID>

<IP‐ADDRESS:ID>

Usage

Route targets should be unique across all VLAN-based (VLANs) and VLAN-aware (bundles) service
instances.  rt  should be manually configured.

Examples

Configuring the import of route target 6800:1.

switch(config-evpn)# vlan-aware-bundle bundle_1

switch(config-evpn-vlan-aware-bundle-bundle_1)# route-target import 6800:1

Removing the import of route target 6800:1.

switch(config-evpn)# vlan-aware-bundle bundle_1

switch(config-evpn-vlan-aware-bundle-bundle_1)# no route-target import

6800:1

Public

route-target (evpn vlan-aware-bundles) 180

Configuring the export of route target 6800:1.

switch(config-evpn)# vlan-aware-bundle bundle_1

switch(config-evpn-vlan-aware-bundle-bundle_1)# route-target export 6800:1

Removing the export of route target 6800:1.

switch(config-evpn)# vlan-aware-bundle bundle_1

switch(config-evpn-vlan-aware-bundle-bundle_1)# no route-target export

6800:1

Configuring the import and export of route target 6800:1.

switch(config-evpn)# vlan-aware-bundle bundle_1

switch(config-evpn-vlan-aware-bundle-bundle_1)# route-target both 6800:1

Removing the import and export of route target 6800:1.

switch(config-evpn)# vlan-aware-bundle bundle_1

switch(config-evpn-vlan-aware-bundle-bundle_1)# no route-target both 6800:1

show arp vlan

Syntax

show arp vlan {VLAN-ID|all}

Description

Displays IPv4 neighbors learnt on L2 VLAN interfaces only when arp-suppression-extended is enabled.

Parameter

{VLAN-ID}

{all}

Usage

Description

Specifies the VLAN ID.

Shows information for all VLANs.

If global arp-suppression-extended is enabled and per-VLAN arp-suppression-extended is disabled,
neighbors learnt on that VLAN are not displayed.

If global arp-suppression-extended is disabled and per-VLAN arp-suppression-extended is enabled,
neighbors learnt on that VLAN are displayed.

Examples

Displaying IPv4 neighbor entries in reachability state for all VLANs:

switch# show arp vlan all

IPv4 Address     MAC                  Vlan       Source

--------------------------------------------------------------
10.10.10.2       00:50:56:bd:27:bc    vlan10     dynamic
10.0.0.2         aa:00:00:00:00:01    vlan20     evpn

Public

show arp vlan 182

Total Number Of ARP Entries Listed: 2.

---------------------------------------------------------------
Displaying IPv4 neighbor entries in reachability state for VLAN 10:

switch# show arp vlan  10

IPv4 Address     MAC                  Vlan       Source

--------------------------------------------------------------

10.10.10.2       00:50:56:bd:27:bc    vlan10     dynamic

Total Number Of ARP Entries Listed: 1.

---------------------------------------------------------------
Displaying IPv4 neighbor entries in reachability state for VLAN 20:
switch# show arp vlan vlan 20

arp-suppression-extended is disabled for vlan 20
Displaying IPv4 neighbor entries in reachability state for VLAN 2:
switch# show arp vlan vlan 2

IPv4 Address     MAC                 Vlan    Source

--------------------------------------------------------------

20.1.1.10        01:02:00:20:20:20   2     dynamic

show evpn ethernet-segment 183

Parameter

{ESI}

Examples

Description

XX.XXXX.XXXX

Showing EVPN ethernet segment information for type 0 and type 1:

(config-lag-if)# show evpn ethernet-segment

ES Port : lag1, ESI : 00ff.ffff.fffe.1111.1111

    Status                     : up

    Mode                       : all-active

    DF Election Wait Time      : 3 seconds

    Route Distinguisher        : 1.1.0.0:0

    RT Import                  : 1111:ffff:0000

    ES Members                 : 3.1.1.2

    Designated Forwarder VLANs : 100,200

ES Port : lag2, ESI : 018c.85c1.4900.0000.0200

    Status                     : up

    Mode                       : all-active

    DF Election Wait Time      : 3 seconds

    Route Distinguisher        : 3.1.1.11:0

    RT Import                  : 0001:1111:1023
    ES Members                 : 3.1.1.2

    Designated Forwarder VLANs : 101,201
Showing EVPN ethernet segment information with es member down for both type 0 and type 1:

(config-lag-if)# show evpn ethernet-segment

ES Port : lag1, ESI : 00ff.ffff.fffe.1111.1111

    Status                     : down, ES port is down

    Mode                       : all-active
    DF Election Wait Time      : 3 seconds
    Route Distinguisher        : 1.1.0.0:0

    RT Import                  : 1111:ffff:0000

    ES Members                 : 3.1.1.2

    Designated Forwarder VLANs :

show evpn vtep-neighbor

Syntax

show evpn vtep-neighbor {all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-Name>}

Description

Shows the remote VTEPs MAC-IP binding. The state of the peer VTEP denotes whether VXLAN tunnel to
the VTEP is Up or Down.

Parameter

all‐vrfs

vrf <vrf‐name>

Description

Display information for all VRFs.

Specify a VRF by VRF name (if no  <VRF‐NAME>   is specifie
d, the default VRF is implied.

Examples

Showing EVPN VTEP neighbor information for all VRFs:

switch# show evpn vtep-neighbor all-vrfs

VTEP-IP           L3VNI  MAC                VRF      State

------------------------------------------------------------------
2.2.2.2           1234   00:20:56:bd:27:bc  VRF1234  Up

2.2.2.2           6789   00:20:56:bd:27:bc  VRF6789  Up

3.3.3.3           1234   00:30:56:ef:aa:cc  VRF1234  Down

4.4.4.4           6789   00:40:56:12:34:44  VRF6789  Up

5.5.5.5           6789   00:50:56:ab:11:ee  VRF6789  Up
Showing EVPN VTEP neighbor information for the specified VRF name:

switch# show evpn vtep-neighbor vrf VRF1234
VTEP-IP           L3VNI  MAC                VRF      State
-----------------------------------------------------------------

2.2.2.2           1234   00:20:56:bd:27:bc  VRF1234  Up

3.3.3.3           1234   00:30:56:ef:aa:cc  VRF1234  Down

show ipv6 neighbor vlan

Syntax

show ipv6 neighbor vlan {<vlan-id>|all}

Public

show ipv6 neighbor vlan 206

Description

Displays a brief view of IPv6 neighbors on L2 VLAN interfaces.

Parameter

{VLAN-ID}

{all}

Usage

Description

Specifies the VLAN ID.

Shows information for all VLANs.

For show ipv6 neighbors vlan all, if EVPN is not enabled, the output is: "The cache is empty with
arp-suppression-extended or nd-suppression-extended disabled."

If global nd-suppression-extended is enabled and per-VLAN nd-suppression-extended is disabled,
neighbors learnt on that VLAN are not displayed.

If global nd-suppression-extended is disabled and per-vlan nd-suppression-extended is enabled,
neighbors learnt on that VLAN are displayed.

For show ipv6 neighbors vlan <vlan-id>, if on vlan-id nd-suppression-extended is disabled, the output is:
"nd-suppression-extended is disabled for vlan <vlan-id>".

Examples

Displaying IPv6 neighbor entries for all VLANs:

switch#  show ipv6 neighbors vlan all

IPv6 Address                 MAC                Vlan      Source

------------------------------------------------------------------

10::2                        00:50:56:bd:27:bc  vlan10    dynamic

2002:1::2                    aa:00:00:00:00:02  vlan20    evpn

Total Number Of IPv6 Neighbors Entries Listed: 2.

------------------------------------------------------------------
Displaying IPv6 neighbor for VLAN 10:

switch# show ipv6 neighbors vlan 10

IPv6 Address                MAC                Vlan      Source

------------------------------------------------------------------

10::2                       00:50:56:bd:27:bc  vlan10    dynamic

Total Number Of IPv6 Neighbors Entries Listed: 1.
------------------------------------------------------------------
Displaying IPv6 neighbor for VLAN 20:
switch# show ipv6 neighbors vlan 20

nd-suppression-extended is disabled for vlan 20

Public

show ipv6 neighbor vlan 207

Displaying IPv6 neighbor for VLAN 2:
switch# show ipv6 neighbors vlan 2

IPv6 Address        MAC                    VLAN   Source

------------------------------------------------------------

20:1::10            01:03:00:20:20:20      2      dynamic

    nd-suppression

vlan 10

        route-target both auto

    vlan 20

        route-target both 3000:1

    vlan-aware-bundle abcd10

        rd 10.10.10.10:10

        route-target export 200.200.200.200:200

    nd-suppression-extended

    vlan 10

        rd 2:2

        route-target both auto

    vlan 20

        rd 3:3

        route-target both 3000:1

    vlan-aware-bundle abcd10

        rd 10.10.10.10:10

        route-target export 200.200.200.200:200

        route-target import 100.100.100.100:100

        redistribute host-route

        vlan 5-6

        vlan-ethernet-tag 5 20

        vlan-ethernet-tag 6 30
