virtual-mac

Syntax

virtual-mac <MAC-ADDR>

no virtual-mac <MAC-ADDR>

Description

Configures the virtual MAC address for EVPN.

The no form of this command removes the virtual MAC address configuration.

Parameter

Description

Specifies the virtual MAC address.

Public

virtual-mac 210

Description

Parameter

<MAC‐ADDR>

Examples

Configuring virtual MAC address for EVPN:

switch(config)# virtual-mac ab:12:33:33:03:22

Removing the configuration of the virtual MAC address for EVPN:

switch(config)# no virtual-mac ab:12:33:33:03:22

NOTE
For EVPN symmetric IRB to work,  virtual-mac  must be configured and it
must be unique for all the VTEPs involved in EVPN except for VSX nodes. In case
of VSX VTEP (logical VTEP), the same  virtual-mac  must be configured
in both the VSX peers. For ease of troubleshooting, it is also recommended to
configure the same value of VSX  system MAC  in VSX VTEP (logical VTEP)
peers.

vlan-aware-bundle

Syntax

vlan-aware-bundle<BUNDLE-NAME>

no vlan-aware-bundle<BUNDLE-NAME>

Public

vlan-aware-bundle 213

Description

Creates the VLAN aware bundle instance and enters the VLAN Aware Bundle context.

The no form of this command removes this configuration.

Parameter

Description

Represents the VLAN Aware Bundle.

<BUNDLE‐NAME>

Usage

VLAN aware bundle and VLAN based service can coexist, but the same VLAN/VNI cannot be part of the
VLAN aware bundle and VLAN based service. The VLAN/VNI part of the VLAN aware bundle should be part
of the VLAN aware bundle on all vteps.

Examples

Creating the VLAN aware bundle instance bundle_1 and entering the VLAN Aware Bundle context.

switch(config-evpn)# vlan-aware-bundle bundle_1

switch(config-evpn-vlan-aware-bundle-bundle_1)#
Removing the VLAN aware bundle instance bundle_1 and exiting the VLAN Aware Bundle context.

switch(config-evpn)# vlan-aware-bundle bundle_1

switch(config-evpn-vlan-aware-bundle-bundle_1)# no vlan-aware-bundle

bundle_1

vlan-ethernet-tag (evpn vlan-aware-bundles)

Syntax

vlan-ethernet-tag <VLAN-ID> {ethernet tag}

no vlan-ethernet-tag <VLAN-ID> {ethernet tag}

Description

Associates the Ethernet Tag ID value to VLANs of the VLAN aware bundles.

The no form of this command removes this configuration.

Parameter

Description

<VLAN‐ID>

{ethernet tag}

Specifies the VLAN to which the ethernet tag is configuring. Ra
nge: 1‐4094.

Specifies the non‐default ethernet tag for the VLAN. Range: 1
‐16777215.

Public

vlan-ethernet-tag (evpn vlan-aware-bundles) 215

Usage

The Ethernet Tag ID value should be unique and associated to only one VLAN of the VLAN aware bundle.
The configuration of this command is not mandatory, but may be required for interoperability with some
third-party vendors.

Examples

Associating the Ethernet tag ID value for vlan aware bundle bundle_1.

switch(config-evpn)# vlan-aware-bundle bundle_1

switch(config-evpn-vlan-aware-bundle-bundle_1)# vlan-ethernet-tag 10 20

Removing the Ethernet tag ID value for vlan aware bundle bundle_1.

switch(config-evpn)# vlan-aware-bundle bundle_1

switch(config-evpn-vlan-aware-bundle-bundle_1)# no vlan-ethernet-tag 10 20

vlan <ID-RANGE>

Syntax

vlan <ID-RANGE>

no vlan <ID-RANGE>

Description

Specifies the list of VLANs that are part of the VLAN Aware Bundle.

The no form of this command removes this configuration.

Parameter

Description

Specifies the VLANS that are part of the bundle. Range: 1‐40
94.

<ID‐RANGE>

Usage

If a VLAN is already part of VLAN based service, it cannot be configured under  vlan-aware-bundle
service and vice versa.

A VLAN cannot be part of more than one EVPN VLAN aware bundles.

Examples

Creating the VLAN aware bundle instance bundle_1 and entering the VLAN Aware Bundle context for vlan
5-10 and 15.

switch(config-evpn)# vlan-aware-bundle bundle_1

switch(config-evpn-vlan-aware-bundle-bundle_1)# vlan 5-10, 15

Removing the VLAN aware bundle instance bundle_1and exiting the VLAN Aware Bundle context for vlan
5-10 and 15.

Public

vlan <ID-RANGE> 217

switch(config-evpn)# vlan-aware-bundle bundle_1

switch(config-evpn-vlan-aware-bundle-bundle_1)# no vlan 5-10, 15

vlan <ID-RANGE> 218

EVPN VXLAN DCI

NOTE

EVPN VXLAN DCI solution is supported only on the 8325/8325P, 8360,
9300/9300S, and 10000 Switch Series.

EVPN VXLAN can be used as a technology to provide Data Center Interconnect (DCI) between two or more
data centers.

Using EVPN VXLAN for DCI provides the following advantages:

•  Both layer 2 (L2) and layer 3 (L3) network connectivity between data centers can be provided over L3

WAN underlay.

•  The underlay need not be aware of overlay VRFs, VLANs, or subnets within the data ceners.

•  Loops are prevented between data centers.

Subtopics

DCI solution details

DCI solution details

EVPN provides network mapping intelligence and enables the VTEPs to exchange endpoint reachability
information with each other. EVPN is used as the overlay control plane, which is a standards-based protocol
that carries both layer 2 and layer 3 information to optimize routing and switching decisions. It uses MP-BGP
for MAC and IP address endpoint distribution. VXLAN tunnels are established between data centers that
carry traffic between the endpoints.

Each data center has VTEPs that establish VXLAN tunnels to the other data centers. The underlay network
for the VXLAN tunnels can be an IP-based network—it would not have any intelligence of the data centers,
but provides reachability for the data centers by using routing protocols. VSX logical VTEP would be used to
provide VTEP high availability.

NOTE
The 8325H Switch series does not support multi-hop IPv6 VXLAN EVPN DCI.

Public

EVPN VXLAN DCI 219

Following are the two solutions for deploying DCI using AOS-CX switches:

•  L2 DCI over VXLAN (Figure 1): L2 DCI over VXLAN refers to the solution that uses only L2 VTEP

functionality.

Figure 1. L2 DCI over VXLAN

•  L3 DCI over VXLAN (Figure 2): L3 DCI over VXLAN refers to the solution that uses both L2 and L3

VTEP functionality. Traffic between data centers can reach other data centers over multiple underlay
links with Equal Cost Multi Pathing (ECMP) as shown in both Figure 1 and Figure 2.

Figure 2. L3 DCI over VXLAN

The following sections describe the various scenarios for VXLAN DCI solution:

•  L2 DCI over VXLAN

•  L3 DCI over VXLAN

Subtopics

L2 DCI over VXLAN

Public

DCI solution details 220

L3 DCI over VXLAN

L2 DCI over VXLAN

L2 DCI over VXLAN is deployed when another external router or firewall exists within each data center
outside the fabric to perform routing. The traffic within same VLAN is L2 switched by the VTEPs, but
inter-VLAN traffic is routed by the external router or firewall. The external router or firewall device could
connect to L2 VTEPs using 802.1Q trunking and VSX LAGs with LACP. VXLAN full mesh tunnels are
established between border leafs of different data centers.

Figure 1 shows an example of L2 DCI over VXLAN along with VRRP on AOS-CX switches. Each data center
has a border VSX L2 VTEP and a external VRRP router to provide overlay routing. The core router is used
for underlay routing over the WAN. If host (1.1.1.1) on VLAN 10 on DC-1 has traffic destined to another
host (1.1.1.20) on VLAN 10 but on DC-3, the traffic would get L2 switched on the tunnel which connects
DC-1 and DC-3. Similarly if a host (2.2.2.2) on VLAN 10 on DC-1 has traffic destined to another host
(1.1.1.10) on VLAN 20 in DC-2, the traffic would be sent on VXLAN tunnel after the VRRP router does the
L3 lookup of traffic in DC-1.

The VRRP solution supported here is active-standby. Only one VRRP router will be active from all three
data centers. If DC-1 has the VRRP Commander, any inter data center routed traffic is routed to the VRRP
commander in DC-1. Routing of traffic will not be local for DC-2 and DC-3.

Figure 1. L2 DCI over VXLAN with VRRP router and external router or firewall device for
underlay connectivity

Public

L2 DCI over VXLAN 221

L3 DCI over VXLAN

L3 DCI over VXLAN can be deployed when distributed L3 gateways are required to provide both L2 and
L3 network connectivity across data centers. Anycast distributed L3 gateways utilize the same MAC or IP
address as default gateway. This way VMs do not need to resend ARP requests if the anycast distributed L3
gateways move from one data center to another. ARP suppression can be enabled on anycast distributed L3
gateways to minimize ARP broadcasts between VTEPs.

In this solution, all switches are VTEPs and additional racks of anycast distributed L3 gateways can be
deployed to expand the L3 DCI over VXLAN solution as shown in Figure 1. This leads to a full mesh of
tunnels between all VTEPs of both VXLAN fabrics. L2 VNI is used to bridge traffic between same subnet or
different VTEP while L3 VNI is used to route between different subnets or VTEPs.

Figure 1. L3 DCI over VXLAN (expanded)

Public

L3 DCI over VXLAN 222

Figure 2 shows an example of L3 DCI over VXLAN. Each data center has a VSX logical VTEP and VLAN or
subnets to be stretched across the data center. The core router is used for underlay routing over the WAN.
VTEPs will have full mesh VXLAN tunnels across data centers.

If host (1.1.1.1) on VLAN 10 on DC-1 on VRF1 has traffic destined to another host (5.5.5.20) on VLAN 50
but on DC-3, the traffic would get routed on the tunnel which is connected to DC-3 over a L3VNI 10000. The
overlay route on the VRF1 would be pointing to a L3 VNI 10000 VXLAN tunnel.

Similarly, if the traffic on VLAN 20 on DC-1 on VRF2 has traffic destined to another host 4.4.4.10 on DC-2,
the overlay route on VRF2 would point to a tunnel with L3 VNI 20000.

If the same VLAN exists in multiple data centers, the same VNI would be used to provide L2 network
connectivity.

Figure 2. L3 DCI over VXLAN

Public

L3 DCI over VXLAN 223

Supported platforms

EVPN VXLAN DCI solution is supported only on the 8325/8325H/8325P, 8360, 9300/9300S, and 10000
Switch Series.

EVPN VXLAN DCI scale information

For information on VXLAN scale information, see 8325 Switch Series Data Sheet, 8360 Switch Series Data
Sheet, 9300 Switch Series Data Sheet, and 10000 Switch Series Data Sheet.

Public

Supported platforms 224

Supported RFCs and standards

The EVPN VXLAN DCI solutions use standards based protocols such as RFC 7348.

Considerations and restrictions

The following considerations and best practices are recommended for EVPN VXLAN DCI:

•  WAN MTU of 1600 bytes and above is recommended. An MTU of 9000 bytes is the ideal value.

The following restrictions are applicable for EVPN VXLAN DCI:

•  Fragmentation and MTU discovery over VXLAN is not supported. Therefore, the WAN path between
VTEPs across data centers are expected to accommodate an increase in MTU of 50 bytes because of
VXLAN encapsulation.

Use cases

The following use cases provide examples of networks using EVPN VXLAN DCI configuration:

•  Use case 1—L2 DCI over VXLAN

•  Use case 2—L3 DCI over VXLAN

•  Use case 3—dual-stack multi-fabric with L2 stretch

Subtopics

Use case 1—L2 DCI over VXLAN
Use case 2—L3 DCI over VXLAN
Use case 3—VXLAN/EVPN Distributed L3 Gateway Configuration with EBGP and OSPF on a Border
VTEP

Use case 1—L2 DCI over VXLAN

In this use case, sample configurations, relevant verification commands for L2 DCI over VXLAN with three
data centers are shown. VSX L2 VTEPs in each data center provides L2 network connectivity for servers and
external routers. IBGPs with the same AS number are used in all data center VTEPs. The underlay inside the
data centers is OSPF, while between the data centers, the underlay is IBGP. An underlay must exist between
the border VTEPs to route the packets between the data centers.

Public

Supported RFCs and standards 225

External routers or firewalls act as gateways for services and applications in the data centers. This release
only supports VRRP routers. The VRRP packets will be synced across data centers using VXLAN tunnels
between the data centers. This solution does not support any other L3 gateway protocols.

Figure 1. Use case 1—L2 DCI over VXLAN

Configuration and verification details for the devices in this use case are provided in the following sections:

•  1B

•  3B

Configuration of switches on DC-1

1A

Public

Use case 1—L2 DCI over VXLAN 226

hostname 1A

!

!

!

vlan 1

vlan 1001

    vsx-sync

vlan 1002

    vsx-sync

evpn

    vlan 1001

        rd auto

        route-target export 65500:1001

        route-target import 65500:1001

    vlan 1002

        rd auto

        route-target export 65500:1002

        route-target import 65500:1002

interface mgmt

    no shutdown

    ip dhcp

system interface-group 1 speed 10g

    !interface group 1 contains ports 1/1/1-1/1/12

system interface-group 3 speed 10g

    !interface group 3 contains ports 1/1/25-1/1/36

interface lag 2 multi-chassis

    no shutdown

    description Servers

    no routing

    vlan trunk native 1

    vlan trunk allowed 1001-1002

    lacp mode active
    lacp rate fast

interface lag 100 multi-chassis

    no shutdown

    description Gateway_Uplink

    no routing

    vlan trunk native 1

    vlan trunk allowed 1001-1002

    lacp mode active

    lacp rate fast

interface lag 255

    no shutdown

Public

Use case 1—L2 DCI over VXLAN 228

ip address 100.100.101.33/32

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

interface vxlan 1

    source ip 100.100.101.3

    vxlan-counters aggregate

    no shutdown

    vni 1001

        vlan 1001

    vni 1002

        vlan 1002

vsx

    inter-switch-link lag 256

    role primary

    keepalive peer 3.3.3.2 source 3.3.3.1

    vsx-sync evpn

!

!

!

!

router ospf 1

    router-id 100.100.101.33

    bfd all-interfaces

    area 0.0.0.0

router bgp 65500

    bgp router-id 100.100.101.33

    no bgp fast-external-fallover

    neighbor 100.100.102.55 remote-as 65500

    neighbor 100.100.102.55 update-source loopback 1

    neighbor 100.100.102.66 remote-as 65500

    neighbor 100.100.102.66 update-source loopback 1

    neighbor 100.100.103.77 remote-as 65500

    neighbor 100.100.103.77 update-source loopback 1
    neighbor 100.100.103.88 remote-as 65500

    neighbor 100.100.103.88 update-source loopback 1

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 100.100.102.55 activate

        neighbor 100.100.102.55 send-community both

        neighbor 100.100.102.66 activate

        neighbor 100.100.102.66 send-community both

        neighbor 100.100.103.77 activate

        neighbor 100.100.103.77 send-community both

        neighbor 100.100.103.88 activate

        neighbor 100.100.103.88 send-community both

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

interface vxlan 1

    source ip 100.100.102.5

    vxlan-counters aggregate

    no shutdown

    vni 1001

        vlan 1001

    vni 1002

        vlan 1002

vsx

    inter-switch-link lag 256

    role secondary

    keepalive peer 5.5.5.1 source 5.5.5.2

    vsx-sync evpn

!

!

!
!

!

router ospf 1

    router-id 100.100.102.66

    bfd all-interfaces

    area 0.0.0.0

router bgp 65500

    bgp router-id 100.100.102.66

    no bgp fast-external-fallover

    neighbor 100.100.101.33 remote-as 65500

    neighbor 100.100.101.33 update-source loopback 1

Public

Use case 1—L2 DCI over VXLAN 240

neighbor 100.100.101.44 remote-as 65500

    neighbor 100.100.103.77 remote-as 65500

    neighbor 100.100.103.77 update-source loopback 1

    neighbor 100.100.103.88 remote-as 65500

    neighbor 100.100.103.88 update-source loopback 1

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 100.100.101.33 activate

        neighbor 100.100.101.33 send-community both

        neighbor 100.100.101.44 activate

        neighbor 100.100.101.44 send-community both

        neighbor 100.100.103.77 activate

        neighbor 100.100.103.77 send-community both

        neighbor 100.100.103.88 activate

        neighbor 100.100.103.88 send-community both

    exit-address-family

!

Configuration of switches on DC-3

3A

hostname 3A

!

!

!

!

!

ssh server vrf mgmt

vlan 1

vlan 1001

    vsx-sync

vlan 1002

    vsx-sync

evpn

    vlan 1001

        rd auto

        route-target export 65500:1001

        route-target import 65500:1001

    vlan 1002

        rd auto

        route-target export 65500:1002

        route-target import 65500:1002

interface mgmt

    no shutdown

    ip dhcp

interface lag 2 multi-chassis

    no shutdown

    description Servers

    no routing

    vlan trunk native 1

    vlan trunk allowed 1001-1002

    lacp mode active

    lacp rate fast

interface lag 100 multi-chassis

Use case 2—L3 DCI over VXLAN

In this use case, sample configurations, relevant verification commands for L3 DCI over VXLAN with three
data centers are shown. Distributed L3 gateway VSX VTEPs in each data center provides L2 or L3 network
connectivity for servers. This use case is based on Symmetric IRB deployment. The underlay inside the data
centers are running OSPF.

Figure 1. Use case 2—L3 DCI over VXLAN

Configuration and verification details for the devices in this use case are provided in the following sections:

•  1A

•  1B

•  Verification of switches on DC-1

Public

Use case 2—L3 DCI over VXLAN 251

•  2A

•  2B

•  Verification of switches on DC-2

•  3A

•  3B

•  Verification of switches on DC-3

Configuration of switches on DC-1

1A

hostname 1A

vrf vrf1

    rd 100.100.101.3:11001

    route-target export 75501:1250 evpn

    route-target import 75501:1250 evpn

!

ssh server vrf mgmt

!

vlan 1

vlan 1001

    vsx-sync

vlan 1002

    vsx-sync

virtual-mac 00:00:00:00:00:33

evpn

    arp-suppression

    vlan 1001

        rd auto

        route-target export 65500:1001

        route-target import 65500:1001

        redistribute host-route

    vlan 1002

        rd auto

        route-target export 65500:1002

        route-target import 65500:1002

        redistribute host-route

    vlan 10

        rd auto

        route-target export 65500:10

        route-target import 65500:10

Public

Use case 2—L3 DCI over VXLAN 252

redistribute host-route

interface mgmt

    no shutdown

    ip dhcp

system interface-group 1 speed 10g

    !interface group 1 contains ports 1/1/1-1/1/12

system interface-group 3 speed 10g

    !interface group 3 contains ports 1/1/25-1/1/36

interface lag 2 multi-chassis

    no shutdown

    description Servers

    no routing

    vlan trunk native 1

    vlan trunk allowed 1001-1002

    lacp mode active

    lacp rate fast

interface lag 255

    no shutdown

    description VSX Keepalive

    ip mtu 9000

    ip address 3.3.3.1/30

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

    ip ospf network point-to-point

interface lag 256

    no shutdown

    no routing

    description VSX LAG

    vlan trunk native 1

    vlan trunk allowed all

interface 1/1/1

    no shutdown

    description WAN
    mtu 9000

    ip mtu 9000

    ip address 192.168.11.2/30

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

    ip ospf network point-to-point

interface 1/1/4

    no shutdown

    mtu 9000

    lag 2

interface 1/1/44

    no shutdown

Public

Use case 2—L3 DCI over VXLAN 253

mtu 9000

    lag 255

interface 1/1/47

    no shutdown

    mtu 9000

    lag 256

interface 1/1/49

    no shutdown

    description WAN

    mtu 9000

    ip mtu 9000

    ip address 192.168.11.17/30

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

    ip ospf network point-to-point

interface loopback 0

    ip address 100.100.101.3/32

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

interface loopback 1

    ip address 100.100.101.33/32

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

interface vlan 1001

    vsx-sync active-gateways

    vrf attach vrf1

    ip mtu 9000

    ip address 10.1.0.13/16

    active-gateway ip mac 00:00:00:dd:ee:ff

    active-gateway ip 10.1.0.254

interface vlan 1002

    vsx-sync active-gateways

    vrf attach vrf1

    ip mtu 9000

    ip address 10.2.0.13/16
    active-gateway ip mac 00:00:00:dd:ee:ff

    active-gateway ip 10.2.0.254

interface vlan 10

    vsx-sync active-gateways

    vrf attach vrf1

    ip mtu 9000

    ip address 10.10.0.13/16

    active-gateway ip mac 00:00:00:dd:ee:ff

    active-gateway ip 10.1.0.254

interface vxlan 1

    source ip 100.100.101.3

Public

Use case 2—L3 DCI over VXLAN 254

no shutdown

    vni 1001

        vlan 1001

    vni 1002

        vlan 1002

    vni 11001

        vrf vrf1

        routing

vsx

    inter-switch-link lag 256

    role primary

    keepalive peer 3.3.3.2 source 3.3.3.1

    vsx-sync bgp evpn

!

!

!

!

!

router ospf 1

    router-id 100.100.101.33

    bfd all-interfaces

    area 0.0.0.0

router bgp 75501

    bgp router-id 100.100.101.33

    no bgp fast-external-fallover

    neighbor 100.100.101.111 remote-as 65500

    neighbor 100.100.101.111 update-source loopback 1

    neighbor 100.100.102.55 remote-as 75502

    neighbor 100.100.102.55 ebgp-multihop 3

    neighbor 100.100.102.55 update-source loopback 1

    neighbor 100.100.102.66 remote-as 75502

    neighbor 100.100.102.66 ebgp-multihop 3
    neighbor 100.100.102.66 update-source loopback 1

    neighbor 100.100.103.77 remote-as 75503

    neighbor 100.100.103.77 ebgp-multihop 3

    neighbor 100.100.103.77 update-source loopback 1

    neighbor 100.100.103.88 remote-as 75503

    neighbor 100.100.103.88 ebgp-multihop 3

    neighbor 100.100.103.88 update-source loopback 1

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 100.100.101.111 activate

        neighbor 100.100.101.111 next-hop-unchanged

        neighbor 100.100.101.111 send-community both

Public

Use case 2—L3 DCI over VXLAN 255

neighbor 100.100.102.55 activate

        neighbor 100.100.102.55 next-hop-unchanged

        neighbor 100.100.102.55 send-community both

        neighbor 100.100.102.66 activate

        neighbor 100.100.102.66 next-hop-unchanged

        neighbor 100.100.102.66 send-community both

        neighbor 100.100.103.77 activate

        neighbor 100.100.103.77 next-hop-unchanged

        neighbor 100.100.103.77 send-community both

        neighbor 100.100.103.88 activate

        neighbor 100.100.103.88 next-hop-unchanged

        neighbor 100.100.103.88 send-community both

    exit-address-family

!

    vrf vrf1

        address-family ipv4 unicast

            redistribute connected

        exit-address-family

!

    router-id 100.100.101.44

    bfd all-interfaces
    area 0.0.0.0

router bgp 75501

    bgp router-id 100.100.101.44

    no bgp fast-external-fallover

    neighbor 100.100.101.111 remote-as 65500

    neighbor 100.100.101.111 update-source loopback 1

    neighbor 100.100.102.55 remote-as 75502

    neighbor 100.100.102.55 ebgp-multihop 3

    neighbor 100.100.102.55 update-source loopback 1

    neighbor 100.100.102.66 remote-as 75502

Configuration of switches on DC-2

2A

hostname 2A

vrf vrf1

    rd 100.100.102.5:11001

    route-target export 75501:1250 evpn

    route-target import 75501:1250 evpn

!

ssh server vrf default

!

vlan 1

vlan 1001

    vsx-sync

vlan 1002

    vsx-sync

virtual-mac 00:00:00:00:00:55

evpn

    arp-suppression

    vlan 1001

        rd auto

        route-target export 65500:1001

        route-target import 65500:1001

        redistribute host-route

    vlan 1002

        rd auto

        route-target export 65500:1002

        route-target import 65500:1002

        redistribute host-route

!

interface mgmt

    no shutdown

    ip dhcp

interface lag 2 multi-chassis

    vsx-sync policies

    no shutdown

    description Servers

    no routing

    vlan trunk native 1

Public

Use case 2—L3 DCI over VXLAN 264

vlan trunk allowed 1001-1002

    lacp mode active

    lacp rate fast

interface lag 255

    no shutdown

    description VSX Keepalive

    ip mtu 9000

    ip address 5.5.5.1/30

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

    ip ospf network point-to-point

interface lag 256

    no shutdown

    no routing

    description VSX LAG

    vlan trunk native 1

    vlan trunk allowed all

interface 1/1/1

    no shutdown

    mtu 9000

    lag 2

interface 1/1/2

    no shutdown

    description WAN

    mtu 9000

    ip mtu 9000

    ip address 192.168.12.2/30

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

    ip ospf network point-to-point

interface 1/1/21

    no shutdown

    mtu 9000

    lag 255
interface 1/1/23

    no shutdown

    mtu 9000

    lag 256

interface loopback 0

    ip address 100.100.102.5/32

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

interface loopback 1

    ip address 100.100.102.55/32

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

interface vlan 1001

Public

Use case 2—L3 DCI over VXLAN 265

vsx-sync active-gateways

    vrf attach vrf1

    ip mtu 9000

    ip address 10.1.0.15/16

    active-gateway ip mac 00:00:00:dd:ee:ff

    active-gateway ip 10.1.0.254

interface vlan 1002

    vsx-sync active-gateways

    vrf attach vrf1

    ip mtu 9000

    ip address 10.2.0.15/16

    active-gateway ip mac 00:00:00:dd:ee:ff

    active-gateway ip 10.2.0.254

interface vxlan 1

    source ip 100.100.102.5

    no shutdown

    vni 1001

        vlan 1001

    vni 1002

        vlan 1002

    vni 11001

        vrf vrf1

        routing

vsx

    inter-switch-link lag 256

    role primary

    keepalive peer 5.5.5.2 source 5.5.5.1

    vsx-sync bgp evpn

!

router ospf 1

    router-id 100.100.102.55

    bfd all-interfaces
    area 0.0.0.0

router bgp 75502

    bgp router-id 100.100.102.55

    no bgp fast-external-fallover

    neighbor 100.100.101.33 remote-as 75501

    neighbor 100.100.101.33 ebgp-multihop 3

    neighbor 100.100.101.33 update-source loopback 1

    neighbor 100.100.101.44 remote-as 75501

    neighbor 100.100.101.44 ebgp-multihop 3

    neighbor 100.100.101.44 update-source loopback 1

    neighbor 100.100.103.77 remote-as 75503

Public

hostname 3A

    route-target import 75501:1250 evpn

!

ssh server vrf default

!

vlan 1

vlan 3

    vsx-sync

vlan 1001

    vsx-sync

vlan 1002

    vsx-sync

virtual-mac 00:00:00:00:00:77

evpn

    arp-suppression

Public

Use case 2—L3 DCI over VXLAN 274

vlan 1001

        rd auto

        route-target export 65500:1001

        redistribute host-route

    vlan 1002

        route-target export 65500:1002

        route-target import 65500:1002

        redistribute host-route

!

interface mgmt

    no shutdown

    ip dhcp

interface lag 2 multi-chassis

    no shutdown

    description Servers

    no routing

    vlan trunk native 1

    vlan trunk allowed 1001-1002

    lacp mode active

    lacp rate fast

interface lag 255

    no shutdown

    description VSX Keepalive

    routing

    ip mtu 9000

    ip address 7.7.7.2/30

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

    ip ospf network point-to-point

interface lag 256

    no shutdown
    no routing

    description VSX LAG

    vlan trunk native 1

    vlan trunk allowed all

interface 1/3/1

    no shutdown

    mtu 9000

    lag 2

interface 1/4/2

    no shutdown

    description WAN

interface vxlan 1

    vni 1002

Use case 2—L3 DCI over VXLAN 276

routing

vsx

    inter-switch-link lag 256

!

!

!

router ospf 1

    router-id 100.100.103.77

    bfd all-interfaces

    redistribute bgp

    area 0.0.0.0

router bgp 75503

    bgp router-id 100.100.103.77

    no bgp fast-external-fallover

    neighbor 100.100.101.33 remote-as 75501

    neighbor 100.100.101.33 ebgp-multihop 3

    neighbor 100.100.101.44 remote-as 75501

    neighbor 100.100.101.44 update-source loopback 1

    neighbor 100.100.102.55 remote-as 75502

    neighbor 100.100.102.55 ebgp-multihop 3

    neighbor 100.100.102.55 update-source loopback 1

    neighbor 100.100.102.66 remote-as 75502

    neighbor 100.100.102.66 ebgp-multihop 3

    neighbor 100.100.102.66 update-source loopback 1

        neighbor 100.100.101.33 activate
        neighbor 100.100.101.33 next-hop-unchanged

        neighbor 100.100.102.66 send-community both

Use case 3—VXLAN/EVPN Distributed L3 Gateway Configuration
with EBGP and OSPF on a Border VTEP

In this use case, the entire campus and on premise edge datacenter is in BGP AS#65100, the campus core
and edge datacenter are in OSPF area 0, campus building#1 is in OSPF area 1.

The VSX 8325 switches in the edge datacenter function as a logical border VTEP to provide connectivity
between the overlay and external/non overlay networks, and on-premises application VMs and a DHCP
server are connected to these 8325 switches. External1 uses /31 links and EBGP to advertise a default
route to the border VXLAN Tunnel Endpoint (VTEP). External2 uses VSX LAG and OSPF area 100 to
advertise 172.16.1.0/24 to the border VTEP.

The border VTEPs are connected via routed-only ports to campus core 8400 switches which function as
EVPN route reflectors (RRs) but not VTEPs. VSX is not mandatory on the 8400 switches but can be enabled
if VSX Link aggregation groups (LAGs) are required to a gateway. The !core 8400 switches are connected
via routed only-ports to aggregate VSX 8320 switches, as VTEP capability is not required at the aggregate
layer.

Aggregate VSX 8320 switches utilize VSX LAGs to access 6300 VSF switches with 802.1Q trunks, and allow
only desired underlay VLANs such as a wireless AP VLAN. VNIs and VLANs linked to the overlay do not
need to be allowed on the aggregate to access VSX LAGs.

Figure 1. Access VTEP, Aggregate Non VTEP, Core EVPN RR, and Border VTEP Topology

Configuration and verification details for the devices in this use case are provided in the following sections:

•  External 1

•  External 2

•  Border VSX, VTEP: 8325-1

•  Border VSX, VTEP: 8325-2

•  Core non VSX, non VTEP, EVPN RR: 8400-1

•  Core non VSX, non VTEP, EVPN RR: 8400-2

•  Agg VSX, non VTEP: 8320-3

Public

Use case 3—VXLAN/EVPN Distributed L3 Gateway Confi... 286

•  Agg VSX, non VTEP: 8320-4

•  Access VSF, VTEP: 6300-1

•  Access VSF, VTEP: 6300-2

Switch Configuration details

External 1

hostname External1

vlan 1,1100,1102

interface vlan1100

    ip address 192.168.3.9/31

    description To 8325-1

interface 1/1/25

    no shutdown

    no routing

    vlan trunk native 1

    vlan trunk allowed 1100

interface vlan1102

    ip address 192.168.3.11/31

    description To 8325-2

interface 1/1/26

    no shutdown

    no routing

    vlan trunk native 1

    vlan trunk allowed 1102

interface loopback 1

    ip address 200.200.200.200/32

    description Connectivity Test Use

router bgp 65001

    bgp fast-external-fallover
    neighbor 192.168.3.8 remote-as 65100

    neighbor 192.168.3.10 remote-as 65100

    address-family ipv4 unicast

        neighbor 192.168.3.8 activate

        neighbor 192.168.3.8 default-originate

        neighbor 192.168.3.10 activate

        neighbor 192.168.3.10 default-originate

! Send default route to border VTEPs

        network 200.200.200.200/32

! Also advertise other networks to verify inbound route-map on border VTEP

works as expected
EBGP peering to border VTEPs.

Neighbor        Remote-AS MsgRcvd MsgSent   Up/Down Time State

AdminStatus

via  192.168.3.10,  [20/0],  bgp

via  192.168.3.8,  [20/0],  bgp
Connectivity works as expected to the devices on the 10.1.10.0/24 and 10.1.11.0/24 overlay subnets.

108 bytes from 10.1.10.20: icmp_seq=3 ttl=62 time=0.357 ms

Border VSX, VTEP: 8325-1

hostname 8325-1

user admin group administrators password ciphertext AQBap!snip

vrf KA

! L3 VRFs should utilize unique Route Distinguisher (RD), the RD is used to

keep all prefixes in the BGP table unique
! It ensures support for overlapping IP addresses and MACs across different

tenants

! Route Target (RT) export and import values should be the same on all

VTEPs with the same VRF

! Different VRFs should utilize different RT values

vrf VRF1

    rd 192.168.1.3:1

    route-target export 65100:1 evpn

    route-target import 65100:1 evpn

! Create additional VRFs if required for multi-tenancy

! e.g. VRF2 can have RD(192.168.1.3:2) and RT(65100:2)

ssh server vrf mgmt

Public

Use case 3—VXLAN/EVPN Distributed L3 Gateway Confi... 290

vlan 1-2

vlan 11

vlan 240

vlan 1100-1101

virtual-mac 02:00:00:00:01:00

! Every distributed L3 gateway VTEP needs to have a unique virtual-mac

! A 8325 VSX logical VTEP is considered 1 VTEP, this VSX pair should share

the same virtual-mac

evpn

    vlan 11

        rd auto

! EVPN RT auto can be used for IBGP EVPN

        route-target export auto

        route-target import auto

        redistribute host-route

! Redistribute host-route is used to advertise /32 host routes to remote

VTEPs

! e.g. 10.1.11.10/32 is connected to 8325-1 and 8325-2

! 10.1.10.20/32 is connected to 6300-1, 10.1.10.21/32 is connected to 6300-2

! This is required as the same subnet is spread across multiple VTEPs

! Without host routes, a remote VTEP would not be aware of the correct

destination VTEP to send traffic

interface mgmt

    no shutdown

    ip static 192.168.40.237/24

    default-gateway 192.168.40.6

system interface-group 1 speed 10g

    !interface group 1 contains ports 1/1/1-1/1/12

system interface-group 2 speed 10g

    !interface group 2 contains ports 1/1/13-1/1/24

system interface-group 4 speed 10g

    !interface group 4 contains ports 1/1/37-1/1/48
! To change ports from default 25G to 10G on 8325

interface lag 1 multi-chassis

    no shutdown

    description Server

    no routing

    vlan trunk native 1

    vlan trunk allowed 11

    lacp mode active

interface 1/1/1

    no shutdown

    mtu 9000

Public

Use case 3—VXLAN/EVPN Distributed L3 Gateway Confi... 291

lag 1

! To server with 10.1.11.10 VM

interface 1/1/3

    no shutdown

    no routing

    vlan access 240

interface vlan 240

    description DHCP server

    ip address 192.168.3.241/29

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

! To DHCP server in VRF default (underlay)

interface vlan 1100

    vrf attach VRF1

    description External1

    ip address 192.168.3.8/31

! To External1

interface 1/1/23

    no shutdown

    no routing

    vlan trunk native 1

    vlan trunk allowed 1100

interface vlan 1101

    vrf attach VRF1

    description External2 and VRF1 IBGP

    ip address 192.168.3.2/29

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.100

! To External2, could be a firewall or switch enabled with OSPF

interface lag 12 multi-chassis

    no routing

    vlan trunk allowed 1101

    lacp mode active

interface 1/1/24

    no shutdown

    lag 12

    vlan trunk allowed all

Use case 3—VXLAN/EVPN Distributed L3 Gateway Confi... 292

lacp mode active

interface 1/1/49

    no shutdown

    mtu 9198

    description VSX ISL link

    lag 256

interface 1/1/50

    no shutdown

    mtu 9198

    description VSX ISL link

    lag 256

! VSX ISL

interface 1/1/41

    no shutdown

    vrf attach KA

    description 8325-2 1/1/41 for keepalive

    ip address 192.168.0.0/31

! VSX KeepAlive

interface 1/1/51

    no shutdown

    mtu 9198

    description 8400-1 1/3/3

    ip mtu 9198

    ip address 192.168.4.1/31

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

    ip ospf network point-to-point

! To Campus Core

interface loopback 0

    ip address 192.168.1.3/32

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

! Used for OSPF and IBGP peering

interface loopback 1
    ip address 192.168.11.3/32

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

! Used for VSX logical VTEP

interface vlan 2

    ip mtu 9198

    ip address 192.168.3.200/31

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

    ip ospf cost 50

    ip ospf network point-to-point

! Transit VLAN to reroute traffic towards Campus Core

interface vlan 11

Public

Use case 3—VXLAN/EVPN Distributed L3 Gateway Confi... 293

vrf attach VRF1

    description Server

    ip mtu 9000

    ip address 10.1.11.1/24

    active-gateway ip mac 12:00:00:00:01:00

    active-gateway ip 10.1.11.1

! Default gateway for server in edge DC

interface vxlan 1

    source ip 192.168.11.3

! Tunnel source using anycast Lo1

    no shutdown

    vni 10011

        vlan 11

    !

    vni 100001

        routing

        vrf VRF1

! L3 VNI for VRF1

vsx

! The same virtual-mac and VSX system-mac are recommended for a VSX logical

VTEP pair

    system-mac 02:00:00:00:01:00

    inter-switch-link lag 256

    role primary

    keepalive peer 192.168.0.1 source 192.168.0.0 vrf KA

ip prefix-list 10-out seq 10 permit 10.1.10.0/24

ip prefix-list 10-out seq 20 permit 10.1.11.0/24

route-map 10-out permit seq 10

     match ip address prefix-list 10-out

! 10-out only allows these /24 to be sent out, if these are not used, /32

host routes will also be advertised

ip prefix-list default-in seq 10 permit 0.0.0.0/0
route-map default-in permit seq 10

     match ip address prefix-list default-in

! default-in only allows the default route to be accepted inbound from

External1

ip prefix-list external_172 seq 10 permit 172.16.1.0/24

route-map ospf-into-bgp permit seq 10

     match ip address prefix-list external_172

! external_172 allows only this subnet to be redistributed from External2

OSPF into BGP

router ospf 1 vrf VRF1

    redistribute connected

Public

Use case 3—VXLAN/EVPN Distributed L3 Gateway Confi... 294

redistribute bgp route-map 10-out

    area 0.0.0.100

! For OSPF with External2, BGP routes are redistributed into OSPF but

only /24 are advertised out

router ospf 1

    router-id 192.168.1.3

    area 0.0.0.0

! For underlay network reachability

router bgp 65100

    bgp router-id 192.168.1.3

    bgp fast-external-fallover

    neighbor campus-core-RR peer-group

    neighbor campus-core-RR remote-as 65100

    neighbor campus-core-RR fall-over

    neighbor campus-core-RR update-source loopback 0

    neighbor 192.168.2.1 peer-group campus-core-RR

    neighbor 192.168.2.2 peer-group campus-core-RR

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor campus-core-RR send-community both

        neighbor 192.168.2.1 activate

        neighbor 192.168.2.2 activate

! IBGP EVPN peering only required to Campus Core RRs

!

    vrf VRF1

        neighbor 192.168.3.3 remote-as 65100

! IBGP IPv4 peering with VSX peer to reroute traffic to External1 should

uplink fail

        neighbor 192.168.3.9 remote-as 65001

! EBGP IPv4 peering with External1, route maps are used to control inbound

and outbound advertisments

        address-family ipv4 unicast

            neighbor 192.168.3.3 activate
            neighbor 192.168.3.9 activate

            neighbor 192.168.3.9 route-map default-in in

            neighbor 192.168.3.9 route-map 10-out out

            redistribute connected

            redistribute ospf route-map ospf-into-bgp

!

https-server vrf mgmt
Useful verification commands:

•  show bgp l2vpn evpn summary

•  show bgp l2vpn evpn

Public

Use case 3—VXLAN/EVPN Distributed L3 Gateway Confi... 295

•  show ip ospf neighbors

•  show ip route

•  show bgp vrf VRF1 ipv4 unicast summary

•  show ip ospf neighbors vrf VRF1

To check tunnels are to remote VTEPs are up, use show interface vxlan.

8325-1# show interface vxlan

Interface vxlan1 is up

Admin state is up

Description:

Underlay VRF: default

Destination UDP port: 4789

VTEP source IPv4 address: 192.168.11.3

VNI        Routing      VLAN   VRF          VTEP Peers        Origin

---------- ------------ ------ ------------ ----------------- --------

10011      disabled     11     --           --                --

100001     enabled      --     VRF1         192.168.2.5       evpn

100001     enabled      --     VRF1         192.168.2.6       evpn

Border VSX, VTEP: 8325-2

hostname 8325-2

user admin group administrators password ciphertext AQBap!snip

vrf KA

! L3 VRFs should utilize unique Route Distinguisher (RD), the RD is used to

keep all prefixes in the BGP table unique

! It ensures support for overlapping IP addresses and MACs across different

tenants

! Route Target (RT) export and import values should be the same on all

VTEPs with the same VRF

! Different VRFs should utilize different RT values

vrf VRF1

    rd 192.168.1.4:1

    route-target export 65100:1 evpn

    route-target import 65100:1 evpn

! Create additional VRFs if required for multi-tenancy

! e.g. VRF2 can have rd(192.168.1.4:2) and RT(65100:2)

ssh server vrf mgmt

vlan 1-2

vlan 11

vlan 1101-1102

virtual-mac 02:00:00:00:01:00
! Every distributed L3 gateway VTEP needs to have a unique virtual-mac

! A 8325 VSX logical VTEP is considered 1 VTEP, this VSX pair should share

the same virtual-mac

evpn

    vlan 11

        rd auto

! EVPN RT auto can be used for IBGP EVPN

        route-target export auto

        route-target import auto

        redistribute host-route

! Redistribute host-route is used to advertise /32 host routes to remote

Public

Use case 3—VXLAN/EVPN Distributed L3 Gateway Confi... 297

VTEPs

! e.g. 10.1.11.10/32 is connected to 8325-1 and 8325-2

! 10.1.10.20/32 is connected to 6300-1, 10.1.10.21/32 is connected to 6300-2

! This is required as the same subnet is spread across multiple VTEPs

! Without host routes, a remote VTEP would not be aware of the correct

destination VTEP to send traffic

interface mgmt

    no shutdown

    ip static 192.168.40.238/24

    default-gateway 192.168.40.6

system interface-group 1 speed 10g

    !interface group 1 contains ports 1/1/1-1/1/12

system interface-group 2 speed 10g

    !interface group 2 contains ports 1/1/13-1/1/24

system interface-group 4 speed 10g

    !interface group 4 contains ports 1/1/37-1/1/48

! To change ports from default 25G to 10G on 8325

interface lag 1 multi-chassis

    no shutdown

    description Server

    no routing

    vlan trunk native 1

    vlan trunk allowed 11

    lacp mode active

interface 1/1/1

    no shutdown

    mtu 9000

    lag 1

! To server with 10.1.11.10 VM

interface vlan 1102

    vrf attach VRF1

    description External1
    ip address 192.168.3.10/31

! To External1

interface 1/1/23

    no shutdown

    no routing

    vlan trunk native 1

    vlan trunk allowed 1102

interface vlan 1101

    vrf attach VRF1

    description External2 and VRF1 IBGP

    ip address 192.168.3.3/29

Use case 3—VXLAN/EVPN Distributed L3 Gateway Confi... 299

ip ospf network point-to-point

! To Campus Core

interface loopback 0

    ip address 192.168.1.4/32

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

! Used for OSPF and IBGP peering

interface loopback 1

    ip address 192.168.11.3/32

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

! Used for VSX logical VTEP

interface vlan 2

    ip mtu 9198

    ip address 192.168.3.201/31

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

    ip ospf cost 50

    ip ospf network point-to-point

! Transit VLAN to reroute traffic towards Campus Core

interface vlan 11

    vrf attach VRF1

    description Server

    ip mtu 9000

    ip address 10.1.11.1/24

    active-gateway ip mac 12:00:00:00:01:00

    active-gateway ip 10.1.11.1

! Default gateway for server in edge DC

interface vxlan 1

    source ip 192.168.11.3

! Tunnel source using anycast Lo1

    no shutdown

    vni 10011

        vlan 11

    !
    vni 100001

        routing

        vrf VRF1

! L3 VNI for VRF1

vsx

! The same virtual-mac and VSX system-mac are recommended for a VSX logical

VTEP pair

    system-mac 02:00:00:00:01:00

    inter-switch-link lag 256

    role secondary

    keepalive peer 192.168.0.0 source 192.168.0.1 vrf KA

Public

Use case 3—VXLAN/EVPN Distributed L3 Gateway Confi... 300

ip prefix-list 10-out seq 10 permit 10.1.10.0/24

ip prefix-list 10-out seq 20 permit 10.1.11.0/24

route-map 10-out permit seq 10

     match ip address prefix-list 10-out

! 10-out only allows these /24 to be sent out, if these are not used, /32

host routes will

! also be advertised

ip prefix-list default-in seq 10 permit 0.0.0.0/0

route-map default-in permit seq 10

     match ip address prefix-list default-in

! default-in only allows the default route to be accepted inbound from

External1

ip prefix-list external_172 seq 10 permit 172.16.1.0/24

route-map ospf-into-bgp permit seq 10

     match ip address prefix-list external_172

! external_172 allows only this subnet to be redistributed from External2

OSPF into BGP

router ospf 1 vrf VRF1

    redistribute connected

    redistribute bgp route-map 10-out

    area 0.0.0.100

! For OSPF with External2, BGP routes are redistributed into OSPF but

only /24 are

! advertised out

router ospf 1

    router-id 192.168.1.4

    area 0.0.0.0

! For underlay network reachability

router bgp 65100

    bgp router-id 192.168.1.4

    bgp fast-external-fallover

    neighbor campus-core-RR peer-group
    neighbor campus-core-RR remote-as 65100

    neighbor campus-core-RR fall-over

    neighbor campus-core-RR update-source loopback 0

    neighbor 192.168.2.1 peer-group campus-core-RR

    neighbor 192.168.2.2 peer-group campus-core-RR

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor campus-core-RR send-community both

        neighbor 192.168.2.1 activate

        neighbor 192.168.2.2 activate

! IBGP EVPN peering only required to Campus Core RRs

!

Public

Use case 3—VXLAN/EVPN Distributed L3 Gateway Confi... 301

vrf VRF1

        neighbor 192.168.3.2 remote-as 65100

! IBGP IPv4 peering with VSX peer to reroute traffic to External1 should

uplink fail

        neighbor 192.168.3.11 remote-as 65001

! EBGP IPv4 peering with External1, route maps are used to control inbound

and outbound advertisments

        address-family ipv4 unicast

            neighbor 192.168.3.2 activate

            neighbor 192.168.3.11 activate

            neighbor 192.168.3.11 route-map default-in in

            neighbor 192.168.3.11 route-map 10-out out

            redistribute connected

            redistribute ospf route-map ospf-into-bgp
Useful verification commands:

•  show bgp l2vpn evpn summary

•  show bgp l2vpn evpn

•  show ip ospf neighbors

•  show ip route

•  show bgp vrf VRF1 ipv4 unicast summary

•  show ip ospf neighbors vrf VRF1

To check tunnels are up to remote VTEPs, use show interface vxlan.

Admin state is up

100001     enabled      --     VRF1         192.168.2.5       evpn

100001     enabled      --     VRF1         192.168.2.6       evpn
To check routes learned from External1, External2 and EVPN /32 host routes, use show ip route vrf VRF1.

Displaying ipv4 routes selected for forwarding

hostname 8320-3

user admin group administrators password ciphertext AQBape!snip

profile L3-agg

vrf keepalive

ssh server vrf mgmt

vlan 1-2,4

spanning-tree

spanning-tree priority 0

spanning-tree config-name Building1

spanning-tree config-revision 1

spanning-tree instance 1 vlan 2,4

! Root bridge for VLANs 2/4

! The network between access and agg switches is loop free as LAGs are used.

! Spanning tree is enabled to provide a backup mechanism to prevent loops
in case it’s

! really required

interface mgmt

    no shutdown

    ip static 192.168.40.239/24

    default-gateway 192.168.40.6

interface lag 1

    vsx-sync vlans

    no shutdown

    description ISL LAG

    no routing

Use case 3—VXLAN/EVPN Distributed L3 Gateway Confi... 313

ip address 192.168.4.9/31

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.1

    no ip ospf passive

    ip ospf network point-to-point

interface loopback 0

    ip address 192.168.2.3/32

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.1

router ospf 1

    router-id 192.168.2.3

    passive-interface default

! OSPF passive default is recommended on switches that connect to client

VLANs but no OSPF

! peers are expected, e.g. OSPF peers are not expected on wireless AP VLAN

    area 0.0.0.1

interface vlan 2

    description management

    ip mtu 9198

    ip address 192.168.12.2/24

    active-gateway ip mac 12:00:00:00:01:00

    active-gateway ip 192.168.12.1

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.1

    no ip ospf passive

! OSPF is enabled on vlan 2 as access VTEPs advertise their loopbacks

    ip ospf priority 255

interface vlan 4

    description wireless

    ip mtu 9198

    ip address 192.168.14.2/24

    active-gateway ip mac 12:00:00:00:01:00

    active-gateway ip 192.168.14.1

    ip helper-address 192.168.3.242

! DHCP relay to DHCP server in the underlay VRF default
    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.1

! OSPF is enabled but set to default passive as OSPF peers are not expected

vsx

    system-mac 00:00:00:01:02:01

    inter-switch-link lag 1

    role primary

    keepalive peer 192.168.0.5 source 192.168.0.4 vrf keepalive

Access VSF, VTEP: 6300-1

hostname 6300M-1-VSF

user admin group administrators password ciphertext AQBap!snip

! L3 VRFs should utilize unique Route Distinguisher (RD), the RD is used to

keep all prefixes in the BGP table unique

! It ensures support for overlapping IP addresses and MACs across different
tenants

! Route Target (RT) export and import values should be the same on all

VTEPs with the same VRF

! Different VRFs should utilize different RT values

Public

Use case 3—VXLAN/EVPN Distributed L3 Gateway Confi... 317

vrf VRF1

    rd 192.168.2.5:1

    route-target export 65100:1 evpn

    route-target import 65100:1 evpn

! Create additional VRFs if required for multi-tenancy

! e.g. VRF2 can have rd(192.168.1.4:2) and RT(65100:2)

!

ssh server vrf default

vsf secondary-member 2

vsf member 1

    type jl661a

    link 1 1/1/51

    link 2 1/1/52

vsf member 2

    type jl662a

    link 1 2/1/27

    link 2 2/1/28

vsf member 3

    type jl668a

    link 1 3/1/27

    link 2 3/1/28

vlan 1-2,4,10

system vlan-client-presence-detect

! For dynamic VLANs

virtual-mac 02:00:00:00:03:00

! Every distributed L3 gateway VTEP needs to have a unique virtual-mac

! A VSF VTEP stack is considered 1 VTEP

evpn

    vlan 10

        rd auto

        route-target export auto

        route-target import auto
! EVPN RT auto can be used for IBGP EVPN

        redistribute host-route

! Redistribute host-route is used to advertise /32 host routes to remote

VTEPs

! e.g. 10.1.11.10/32 is connected to 8325-1 and 8325-2

! 10.1.10.20/32 is connected to 6300-1, 10.1.10.21/32 is connected to 6300-2

! This is required as the same subnet is spread across multiple VTEPs

! Without host routes, a remote VTEP would not be aware of the correct

destination VTEP to send traffic

spanning-tree

spanning-tree config-name Building1

Public

Use case 3—VXLAN/EVPN Distributed L3 Gateway Confi... 318

spanning-tree config-revision 1

spanning-tree instance 1 vlan 2,4

! The network between access and agg switches is loop free as LAGs are used.

! Spanning tree is enabled to provide a backup mechanism to prevent loops

in case it’s really required

interface lag 2

    no shutdown

    description Uplink to Agg

    no routing

    vlan trunk native 1

    vlan trunk allowed 2,4

    lacp mode active

interface 1/1/49

    no shutdown

    mtu 9198

    lag 2

interface 2/1/25

    no shutdown

    mtu 9198

    lag 2

interface 1/1/1

    no shutdown

    no routing

    vlan access 10

! To client on VLAN 10

interface 2/1/1

    no shutdown

    no routing

    vlan access 4

! To wireless AP

interface loopback 0

    ip address 192.168.2.5/32
    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.1

! VTEP source and inband mgmt IP

router ospf 1

    router-id 192.168.2.5

    passive-interface default

    area 0.0.0.1

! OSPF passive-interface default is recommended on switches that connect to

client VLANs but

! no OSPF peers are expected, e.g. OSPF peers are not expected on wireless

AP VLAN

interface vlan 2

Public

Use case 3—VXLAN/EVPN Distributed L3 Gateway Confi... 319

ip mtu 9198

    ip address 192.168.12.5/24

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.1

    no ip ospf passive

! OSPF used to share VTEP source and inband mgmt IP with Agg switches

interface vlan 10

    vrf attach VRF1

    ip address 10.1.10.1/24

    active-gateway ip mac 12:00:00:00:01:00

    active-gateway ip 10.1.10.1

! Active-gateway configs provide the anycast L3 distributed gateway

capability

interface vxlan 1

    source ip 192.168.2.5

    no shutdown

    vni 10010

        vlan 10

    vni 100001

        routing

        vrf VRF1

! L3 VNI for VRF1

router bgp 65100

    bgp router-id 192.168.2.5

    neighbor campus-core-RR peer-group

    neighbor campus-core-RR remote-as 65100

    neighbor campus-core-RR fall-over

    neighbor campus-core-RR update-source loopback 0

    neighbor 192.168.2.1 peer-group campus-core-RR

    neighbor 192.168.2.2 peer-group campus-core-RR

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor campus-core-RR send-community both

        neighbor 192.168.2.1 activate
        neighbor 192.168.2.2 activate

!

    vrf VRF1

        address-family ipv4 unicast

            redistribute connected

! Redistributed connected is to advertise EVPN type 5 (IP prefix) route to

remote VTEPs

!

https-server vrf default
Useful verification commands:

•  show lacp interfaces

Public

•  show spanning-tree

•  show mac-address-table

To check tunnels are up to remote VTEPs, use the command show interface vxlan.

Interface vxlan1 is up

Admin state is up

Underlay VRF: default

Destination UDP port: 4789

100001     enabled      --     VRF1         192.168.2.6       evpn

100001     enabled      --     VRF1         192.168.11.3      evpn
To check routes learned from External1, External2 and EVPN /32 host routes, use the command show bgp
l2vpn evpn.

multipath,

Displaying ipv4 routes selected for forwarding

'[x/y]' denotes [distance/metric]

Access VSF, VTEP: 6300-2

hostname 6300M-2-VSF

user admin group administrators password ciphertext AQBap!snip

! L3 VRFs should utilize unique Route Distinguisher (RD), the RD is used to

keep all prefixes in the BGP table unique
! It ensures support for overlapping IP addresses and MACs across different
tenants

! Route Target (RT) export and import values should be the same on all

VTEPs with the same VRF

Public

Use case 3—VXLAN/EVPN Distributed L3 Gateway Confi... 325

! Different VRFs should utilize different RT values

vrf VRF1

    rd 192.168.2.6:1

    route-target export 65100:1 evpn

    route-target import 65100:1 evpn

! Create additional VRFs if required for multi-tenancy

! e.g. VRF2 can have rd(192.168.1.4:2) and RT(65100:2)

!

ssh server vrf default

vsf secondary-member 2

vsf member 1

    type jl661a

    link 1 1/1/51-1/1/52

vsf member 2

    type jl662a

    link 1 2/1/27-2/1/28

vlan 1-2,4,10

system vlan-client-presence-detect

! For dynamic VLANs

virtual-mac 02:00:00:00:04:00

! Every distributed L3 gateway VTEP needs to have a unique virtual-mac

! A VSF VTEP stack is considered 1 VTEP

evpn

    vlan 10

        rd auto

        route-target export auto

        route-target import auto

! EVPN RT auto can be used for IBGP EVPN

        redistribute host-route

! Redistribute host-route is used to advertise /32 host routes to remote

VTEPs

! e.g. 10.1.11.10/32 is connected to 8325-1 and 8325-2
! 10.1.10.20/32 is connected to 6300-1, 10.1.10.21/32 is connected to 6300-2

! This is required as the same subnet is spread across multiple VTEPs

! Without host routes, a remote VTEP would not be aware of the correct

destination VTEP to send traffic

spanning-tree

spanning-tree config-name Building1

spanning-tree config-revision 1

spanning-tree instance 1 vlan 2,4

interface lag 2

    no shutdown

    description Uplink to Agg

Public

Use case 3—VXLAN/EVPN Distributed L3 Gateway Confi... 326

no routing

    vlan trunk native 1

    vlan trunk allowed 2,4

    lacp mode active

interface 1/1/49

    no shutdown

    mtu 9198

    lag 2

interface 2/1/25

    no shutdown

    mtu 9198

    lag 2

interface 1/1/1

    no shutdown

    no routing

    vlan access 10

! To client on VLAN 10

interface 2/1/1

    no shutdown

    no routing

    vlan access 4

! To wireless AP

interface loopback 0

    ip address 192.168.2.6/32

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.1

! VTEP source and inband mgmt IP

router ospf 1

    router-id 192.168.2.6

    passive-interface default

    area 0.0.0.1

! OSPF passive-interface default is recommended on switches that connect to

client VLANs but
! no OSPF peers are expected, e.g. OSPF peers are not expected on wireless

AP VLAN

interface vlan 2

    ip mtu 9198

    ip address 192.168.12.6/24

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.1

    no ip ospf passive

! OSPF used to share VTEP source and inband mgmt IP with Agg switches

interface vlan 10

    vrf attach VRF1

    ip address 10.1.10.1/24

Public

Use case 3—VXLAN/EVPN Distributed L3 Gateway Confi... 327

active-gateway ip mac 12:00:00:00:01:00

    active-gateway ip 10.1.10.1

! Active-gateway configs provide the anycast L3 distributed gateway

capability

interface vxlan 1

    source ip 192.168.2.6

    no shutdown

    vni 10010

        vlan 10

    vni 100001

        routing

        vrf VRF1

! L3 VNI for VRF1

router bgp 65100

    bgp router-id 192.168.2.6

    neighbor campus-core-RR peer-group

    neighbor campus-core-RR remote-as 65100

    neighbor campus-core-RR fall-over

    neighbor campus-core-RR update-source loopback 0

    neighbor 192.168.2.1 peer-group campus-core-RR

    neighbor 192.168.2.2 peer-group campus-core-RR

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor campus-core-RR send-community both

        neighbor 192.168.2.1 activate

        neighbor 192.168.2.2 activate

!

    vrf VRF1

        address-family ipv4 unicast

            redistribute connected

! Redistributed connected is to advertise EVPN type 5 (IP prefix) route to

remote VTEPs

!
https-server vrf default

FAQs

What is the recommended network latency between data centers?

The network latency is dependent on application latency requirements.

For example, the network might be able to support 100-200 ms network latency between data centers, but
applications between the data centers might not support this value.

Does the solution require EVPN control plane?

Yes, the DCI solution requires EVPN based control plane. The DCI solution does not support static VXLAN.

Does the solution support multicast traffic over VXLAN?

Yes, see AOS-CX Multicast Guide for Multicast VXLAN configurations that you can add.

References

Public

FAQs 333

•  AOS-CX EVPN VXLAN Guide

•  AOS-CX Multicast Guide

•  AOS-CX EVPN VXLAN Symmetric IRB Distributed L3 Gateways

•  AOS-CX Switch Simulator

EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric solution

This chapter describes the supported topologies and features of the EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric solution,
which extends the basic single-fabric EVPN VXLAN solution to support multi-fabric deployments.

NOTE
This feature is not supported on 5420 or 6200 Switch series.

A Data Center (DC) network can be divided into multiple fabrics due the following reasons:

•  DCs are geographically distributed.

•  Scale—There is a limit to the number of supported VTEPs per fabric. When VTEP scale increases, it is

recommended to split a single fabric into multiple fabrics.

•  Simplified management of the network—A single DC can also be split into multiple fabrics based on

logical functions (for example, QA PoD (Point of Delivery)/fabric, Production PoD/fabric) for simplified
management.

•  Security—DCs can be split into multiple fabrics with perimeter firewalls/security policies.

Multiple fabrics are interconnected by an IP network and 2 types of multi-fabrics are supported:

•  L3 multi-fabric: Inter-fabric overlay traffic has to be routed while intra-fabric traffic can be bridged or

routed. In other words tenant IP subnets do not stretch beyond fabrics.

•  L2 multi-fabric: At least one tenant subnet stretches across two or more fabrics. In DCs, tenant subnet
stretch across fabrics is a very critical requirement. Geographical redundancy and VM migration are the
common use cases demanding subnet stretch.

•  Both L2 and L3 multi-fabric can be enabled at the same time; they are not mutually-exclusive.

The technology used in this solution is applicable to both DC and Campus networks.

For more details, refer to the resources listed in the References section.

EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric solution overview

This section provides a brief description of the single-hop single VXLAN fabric solution before discussing
the multi-fabric solution in greater detail.

As shown below, the terms campus zone, DC, and site are used interchangeably depending on the
environment.

Even though a single VTEP is shown in the diagrams below, VSX VTEPs for device redundancy should be
utilized where applicable.

Single-hop single VXLAN fabric

Figure 1. Single-hop single VXLAN fabric across DCs/sites

The single-hop VXLAN solution with a single fabric provides the following:

•  Full mesh of VXLAN tunnels between VTEPs within a VXLAN fabric

•  A single VXLAN fabric with limited VTEP scale (128 VTEPs recommended)

EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric with single fabric per site

Figure 2. Multi-hop VXLAN with multiple fabrics across DCs/sites

Public

EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric solution overview 337

The EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric solution with single fabric per site provides the following:

•  Full mesh of VXLAN tunnels between VTEPs within a VXLAN fabric

•  VTEPs between different fabrics avoid full mesh of tunnels and BGP peering between fabrics

•  Border VTEPs establish VXLAN tunnels between fabrics. A full mesh is required between border VTEPs.

•  Border VTEPs provide tunnel-to-tunnel forwarding (intermediate VTEP hopping) for L2/L3 traffic

between fabrics and prevent loops between fabrics

•  Multiple VXLAN fabrics across sites provide improved VTEP scale

EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric with multiple fabrics per site

Figure 3. EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric with multiple fabrics per site - eBGP control plane

Public

EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric solution overview 338

Figure 4. EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric with multiple fabrics per site - VXLAN data plane

In environments that require multiple fabrics per site (e.g. multiple pods in a DC), the EVPN VXLAN
multi-fabric solution with multiple fabrics per site provides the following:

Public

EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric solution overview 339

•  Full mesh of VXLAN tunnels between VTEPs within a VXLAN fabric

•  VTEPs between different fabrics avoid full mesh of tunnels and BGP peering between fabrics

•  The concept of a border leader VTEP is introduced (1 per site), this border leader VTEP will eBGP peer
to all border VTEPs within the same site and also eBGP peer with border leaders in other sites as shown
in Figure 3

◦  8325 can be deployed as border leader VTEP (e.g. Fabric1)

◦  8325 or 8360 can be deployed as border VTEP (e.g. Fabric11)

◦  Any CX switch platform that supports EVPN VXLAN can be deployed as VTEPs within a fabric

•  The border leader VTEP will also function as border VTEP for it's own fabric

•  Border leader and border VTEPs automatically establish VXLAN tunnels with other border leader and
border VTEPs with the help of EVPN control plane. A full mesh of VXLAN tunnels is required between
them as shown in Figure 4.

•  Border leader and border VTEPs provide tunnel-to-tunnel forwarding (intermediate VTEP hopping) for

L2/L3 traffic between fabrics and prevent loops between fabrics

•  Multiple VXLAN fabrics provide improved VTEP scale to go beyond the limit of number VTEPs per

fabric.

Related features

L2 VXLAN—selective split-horizon enforcement

Figure 5. L2 split horizon in VXLAN

Default split-horizon behavior in VXLAN switches

By default, VXLAN networks require full-mesh connectivity between VTEPs. Consider the full mesh single-
fabric network shown in Figure 5 above. For example, Host H1 originates a BUM packet and one copy of
this packet reaches Switch S2 and Switch S3 over the direct VXLAN tunnel from Switch S1 on the L2VNI
corresponding to VLAN 10. Now if Switch S2 and Switch S3 forward this packet on the same L2VNI to
Switch S3 and Switch S2 respectively, a loop is formed in the overlay. Therefore, VTEPs perform split horizon

Public

EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric solution overview 340

and do not perform multi-fabric VXLAN bridging by default; VXLAN bridging between VXLAN tunnels is
prohibited.

The full mesh assumption is valid when it comes multi-hop VXLAN bridging between intra-fabric and
inter-fabric VXLAN tunnels. Here, split horizon enforcement should not be performed in order to deliver
BUM traffic from one fabric to another. Therefore, the border VTEP has to enforce split horizon selectively as
shown in Figure 6 below.

Figure 6. Selective split horizon in border VTEPs

Such selective split horizon can be enforced (in supported platforms) with additional configurations. This
is achieved by associating by allowing traffic between intra-fabric and inter-fabric tunnels only. The switch
enforces split horizon when VXLAN bridging between tunnels belonging to the same broadcast domain and
disable split horizon enforcement across different broadcast domains. By associating all intra-fabric tunnels
to one broadcast domain and all inter-fabric tunnels to another, the desired selective split horizon behavior
is achieved.

NOTE
Broadcast domain association is not per L2VNI, it is per VXLAN tunnel - the local
and remote VTEP pair.

To change the default behavior, the command  dyn-vxlan-tunnel-bridging-mode ibgp-eb
gp  is available to disable split horizon between iBGP learned VXLAN tunnels and eBGP learned VXLAN
tunnels. This mandates the use of iBGP within a fabric and eBGP between fabrics.

EVPN route-map

Public

EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric solution overview 341

The EVPN route map provides the following:

•  Provides EVPN route filtering support for VXLAN deployments

•  Allows a VTEP to apply an inbound or/and outbound route-map to a BGP peer under EVPN address-

family context

◦  Match conditions: IPv4/IPv6 address prefix-list, aspath-list, VNI (L2/L3)

◦  Set clauses: local-preference, as-path prepend/exclude, IP next-hop

•  Required when EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric is deployed to minimize the number of routes

Figure 7 shows an example with the objective and solution provided by the EVPN route map.

Figure 7. EVPN route map

Objective: Border VTEP2 should be preferred as outbound VTEP over Border VTEP1 for routes towards
AS#65100

Solutions:

•  On VTEP2, set higher local-preference for EVPN routes learned from AS#65100 and lower local

preference on VTEP1 for AS#65100 routes.

•  Set AS-path prepending on VTEP1 for EVPN routes learned from AS#65100.

EVPN next-hop-self

Public

EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric solution overview 342

EVPN next-hop-self provides the ability to modify the next-hop VTEP IP address.

Figure 8 shows an example where VTEPs in AS#65001 use border leader VTEP (1.1.1.1/32) as the
next-hop VTEP IP address to establish VXLAN tunnels since the expectation is for VTEPs within a fabric not
to establish VXLAN tunnels with VTEPs in other fabrics.

Figure 8. EVPN next-hop-self

EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric solution details

As shown below, campus zone/DC/site terminology are used interchangeably depending on the
environment.

Even though a single VTEP is shown in the diagrams below, VSX VTEPs for device redundancy should be
utilized where applicable.

EVPN route-map with a single fabric per site

Figure 1. EVPN route-map with a single fabric per site

Public

EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric solution details 343

•

If single fabric per site is desired, the border VTEP at each site will configure eBGP peering and
outbound EVPN route map towards remote border VTEPs in other sites.

•  The route map will match and allow EVPN routes/MACs from it's Local-AS (^$), this will prevent

unnecessary routes from being advertised out.

EVPN route-map with multiple fabrics per site

Figure 2. EVPN route-map with multiple fabrics per site

If multiple fabrics per site is desired:

•  The border leader VTEP at each site will eBGP peer and apply outbound EVPN route map towards

remote border leader VTEPs in remote sites

◦  Using Figure 2 as an example, border leader VTEP1 will have an outbound route-map towards other
border leader VTEPs in remote sites, match on AS#65011 and set next-hop IP to 1.1.1.11 (border
VTEP of AS#65011 fabric) so that other remote border VTEPs are aware of the correct next-hop IP
to build the VXLAN tunnel and forward traffic

◦  Border leader VTEP1 will also have an entry in the outbound route-map towards other border leader
VTEPs in remote sites to match on it's Local-AS (^$) and set next-hop IP to 1.1.1.1 (border VTEP of
AS#65001 fabric) so that other remote border VTEPs are aware of the correct next-hop IP to build
the VXLAN tunnel and forward traffic while preventing unnecessary routes from being advertised
out

•  The border leader VTEP at each site will eBGP peer and apply outbound EVPN route map towards

remote border VTEPs in the same site

◦  Using Figure 2 as an example, border leader VTEP1 will have an outbound route-map towards other
border VTEPs in the same site, match on AS#65012 and set next-hop IP to 1.1.1.12 (border VTEP

Public

EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric solution details 344

of AS#65012 fabric) so that border VTEPs in site1 are aware of the correct next-hop IP to build the
VXLAN tunnel and forward traffic

EVPN next-hop-self

Figure 3. EVPN next-hop-self within each fabric

•  Within each fabric, the border VTEP will set EVPN next-hop-self towards other VTEPs.

•  This will ensure VTEPs within a fabric will build VXLAN tunnels towards the border VTEPs and not to

VTEPs in other fabrics.

•

It also replaces the router-mac extended community of EVPN routes with its own MAC.

L3 multi-fabric forwarding

Figure 4. L3 multi-fabric forwarding for routed traffic

Public

EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric solution details 345

•  This is applicable to both single fabric per site and multiple fabrics per site.

•  Assuming EVPN control plane and VXLAN tunnels are active between all VTEPs, host routes are

advertised and learned.

•  When Host1 (connected to VTEP1) sends traffic to Host2 (connected to VTEP4):

1.  VTEP1 sees destination IP in VRF1 route table with next-hop IP of Border-VTEP2 via L3VNI

of VRF1VTEP1 encapsulates and forwards traffic into VXLAN tunnel with next-hop IP of Border-
VTEP2

2.  Border-VTEP2 sees destination IP in VRF route table with next-hop IP of Border-VTEP3 via L3VNI
of VRF1Border-VTEP2 performs tunnel to tunnel forwarding with next-hop IP of Border-VTEP3

3.  Border-VTEP3 sees destination IP in VRF route table with next-hop IP of VTEP4 via L3VNI of

VRF1Border-VTEP3 performs tunnel to tunnel forwarding with next-hop IP of VTEP4

4.  VTEP4 sees destination IP in VRF route table with interface/port of directly connected host.VTEP4

performs VXLAN decapsulation and forwards traffic to Host2

•  L3 EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric capability is only required on Border-VTEPs

•  VTEP1 and VTEP4 could be VTEPs that do not support EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric

L2 multi-fabric forwarding

Figure 5. L2 multi-fabric forwarding for bridged traffic

Public

EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric solution details 346

•  This is applicable to both single fabric per site and multiple fabrics per site

•  VXLAN split horizon is enabled by default for BUM traffic

◦  L2 tunnel to tunnel forwarding requires disabling VXLAN split-horizon between iBGP and eBGP

broadcast groups on Border-VTEPs

•  Assuming EVPN control plane and VXLAN tunnels are established between all VTEPs, MAC addresses

are advertised and learned

•  When Host1 connected to VTEP1 sends traffic to Host2 connected to VTEP4:

1.  VTEP1 sees destination MAC in MAC table with next-hop IP of Border-VTEP2 via L2VNI VTEP1

encapsulates and forwards traffic into VXLAN tunnel with next-hop IP of Border-VTEP2

2.  Border VTEP2 sees destination MAC in MAC table with next-hop IP of Border VTEP3 via L2VNI

Border VTEP2 performs tunnel to tunnel forwarding with next-hop IP of Border VTEP3

3.  Border VTEP3 sees destination MAC in MAC table with next-hop IP of VTEP4 via L2VNIBorder

VTEP3 performs tunnel to tunnel forwarding with next-hop IP of VTEP4

4.  VTEP4 sees destination MAC in MAC table with interface/port of directly connected host.VTEP4

performs VXLAN decapsulation and forwards traffic to Host2

•  L2 EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric capability is only required on Border VTEPs

•  VTEP1 and VTEP4 could be VTEPs that do not support EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric

Supported platform

The EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric solution is supported only with the 8325, 8325H and 10000* Switch Series
as a border-leader VTEP.

Public

Supported platform 347

Border VTEPs (but non-border-leaders) that support this solution include the 8325, 8360, and 10000
Switch Series.

Non-border VTEPS that support this solution include the 6300, 6400, 8325, 8360, 8400, and 10000 Switch
Series.

When the 10000 switch series device is configured as a Border-VTEP or Border Leader, tunnel-to-tunnel
traffic is dropped by default. Enabling uplink-to-uplink mode allows tunnel-to-tunnel traffic to be
forwarded. Firewall policies do not apply to tunnel-to-tunnel traffic when uplink-to-uplink mode is enabled.

VXLAN-EVPN multi-fabric scale information

The following table provides scale information for the VXLAN-EVPN multi-fabric solution.

Table 1. VXLAN‐EVPN multi‐fabric scale

Border Leader VTEP

8325/8325P

HW profile

VTEPs per fabric (standalone or VSX logical VTEP p
air)

Sites (Number of VSX border‐leader VTEPs)

Fabrics across sites (Number of VSX border‐VTEPs
, VXLAN full‐mesh)

L2 VNI

L3 VNI

Leaf

64
